Mr Voss is there too, immaculate except for a tiny blood spot on one cuff and the polished brogues Nell saw earlier. Mr Voss does not behave like a villain in a melodrama. He behaves like an administrator inconvenienced by weather. He says Ollie has had an accident. He says the students are distressed and confused. He says any online rumours must be handled “through proper channels.” He says they must all return to the sports hall and get some sleep before the morning’s celebration event. Tilly explodes. Nell, for once, does not shrink. Mr Voss quietly threatens them with exclusions, police involvement, damage to GCSE references, and “serious safeguarding consequences” if they spread misinformation. He frames silence as maturity. Ollie says nothing. For the first time, he looks less golden boy than frightened boy who started a fire and realised adults are better arsonists. The chapter ends with Mr Voss taking Nell’s hidden phone from her hand and saying, “No more little broadcasts.” ## Chapter 7 — Assembly of Lies Morning arrives grey, damp, and aggressively British. The school begins its donor breakfast as if nothing happened. Parents, governors, academy trust visitors, and a local journalist gather in the assembly hall. There are croissants from Costco, a banner reading **KINDNESS IS OUR CULTURE**, and a slideshow of smiling students who have mostly not consented to being inspirational. Nell, Tilly, Jay, and Bea have been warned to stay quiet. Ollie is absent, supposedly taken home with a “minor sports injury.” Mr Voss prepares to give a speech about digital resilience and student voice. The students seem defeated at first. Nell is furious with herself, certain she has failed again. Then she notices something: Bea still has the drama department’s ancient camcorder, used for recording rehearsals. It was running in the studio before the lights went out. The footage is grainy and badly framed, but it captured enough: - Tilly confronting Ollie. - Ollie taunting her. - The door opening. - Mr Voss entering. - Ollie panicking and stepping backwards. - Ollie tripping over the stage block and hitting his head. - Mr Voss picking up Ollie’s phone instead of immediately helping him. It was an accident, but the cover-up was a choice. Nell has to decide whether to expose everything publicly, knowing it will also reveal parts of Tilly’s private life and other students’ secrets. Tilly, shaking, tells her: “If they’re going to tell it anyway, we tell it first.” During Mr Voss’s speech, Nell walks to the AV desk with Bea. Jay creates a distraction involving the fire alarm panel and a tray of orange juice. Tilly stands beside Nell. They play the footage. Chaos follows. Mr Voss tries to stop it. Parents gasp. The journalist starts recording. Students begin speaking up from the audience, one after another, naming Saints Unfiltered and what it did to them. Nell sees her chance to disappear into silence as usual. Instead, she takes the microphone and admits she knew Tilly was being targeted before and did nothing. Her voice shakes. It is not heroic. It is awkward, too loud, and accompanied by feedback. But it is the truth. The chapter ends with Tilly taking the microphone from Nell and saying, “Right. Since we’re apparently doing student voice…” ## Chapter 8 — Aftercare, Academy-Style The aftermath is messy rather than magical. An ambulance comes for Ollie, who will be fine but less photogenic for a while. Police arrive, though mostly they look annoyed to be dealing with a school that has turned a wellbeing event into a hostage situation with pastries. Parents flood reception. The academy trust issues a statement before lunch using the words **isolated**, **robust**, and **community healing**, which Nell thinks should legally count as a confession. Mr Voss is placed on leave pending investigation. Saint Bart’s claims it cannot comment further, then comments further in six separate emails. Saints Unfiltered is taken down, but screenshots live on, because the internet is a cockroach in a hoodie. Still, the spell is broken. The anonymous monster has names, emails, timestamps, and adult fingerprints. Nell faces consequences too. Her mum is furious about the hidden phone, the sneaking around, and the fact that Nell’s first instinct in a crisis was apparently “light burglary.” But she also hugs her so hard Nell can barely breathe. Tilly and Nell sit outside school on a damp wall beside the bins, wrapped in emergency foil blankets that make them look like budget baked potatoes. Their reconciliation is not instant. Tilly does not pretend everything is forgiven. Nell does not ask her to. Nell apologises properly, without excuses. Tilly accepts it partially, which is more than Nell deserves and exactly enough for now. They joke about the pads used to save Ollie’s head wound. Tilly says Saint Bart’s should put that in the newsletter: **PERIOD PRODUCTS: NOT JUST FOR PERIODS, ACTUALLY ESSENTIAL HEALTHCARE, YOU ABSOLUTE TURNIPS.** Nell laughs for the first time since midnight. Later, as they are finally allowed to leave, Nell’s phone buzzes. It is a message from Tilly. **🩸🩸🩸** Nell looks up, alarmed. Tilly, standing three feet away, deadpan, says she has actually started her period now and has bled through the emergency leggings during a police interview, because apparently the universe has a commitment to structure. Nell digs in her bag. This time, she is ready.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# Human Approach A human writer approaching the prompt — **“The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸”** — would probably begin less with “What is the optimal plot architecture?” and more with a gut reaction. They might ask: - Who sends a text like that? - Why emojis instead of words? - Is it funny, frightening, melodramatic, or all three? - What does the recipient *think* it means? - What does it *actually* mean? - What sort of person sees three blood drops at midnight and still makes a joke? A human writer might latch onto the ambiguity of the emojis first. Three blood drops are very teenage, very digital, slightly ridiculous, and genuinely alarming. They carry melodrama. They might mean murder, menstruation, a prank, self-harm, a horror-game signal, a group-chat in-joke, or just “omg disaster.” That tension between the emoji’s childishness and the blood’s seriousness is the best part of the prompt. A human YA writer, especially one leaning darkly comic and British, would likely focus on **voice before plot**. The plot matters, but the piece will live or die on whether the narrator sounds like an actual sharp teenage girl rather than an adult explaining a sharp teenage girl. The first page needs to feel like someone funny, tired, annoyed, and scared is telling us this because she has no choice. A human might also be more willing to make the story awkward, messy, and specific: - The school floor smells of old trainers and disinfectant. - Someone’s sleeping bag has a Fortnite logo despite them being sixteen. - The narrator’s phone battery is on 7% because she spent the evening hate-scrolling someone’s Instagram. - The “charity lock-in” is not a generic YA event but something recognisably British and depressing: a sponsored sleepout for “mental health awareness” where the school has provided squash in plastic jugs and one teacher keeps saying “hydrate, guys.” - The blood emojis might have a private history that is stupid rather than poetic. - The narrator might not respond nobly; she might think about ignoring the text for thirty seconds because friendship is complicated and she is tired and cowardly. A human writer might resist making every plot element thematically tidy. They might let some odd, unnecessary, memorable details exist because life is like that. A student might be dressed as a banana because of a charity dare. The caretaker might be called Big Keith and have a weird obsession with laminated notices. Someone might keep offering everyone Mini Cheddars at the worst possible moments. These details may not “advance the plot,” but they create texture and humanity. A human might also preserve discomfort. If the narrator failed her friend in the past, the apology might not fix it. The friend might still be mean to her. The narrator might do the right thing partly because she wants to stop feeling like a coward, not purely because she has achieved moral clarity. A human writer often understands that motives are mixed. The best human version of this piece would probably be: - Less neat. - More voice-led. - More surprising in its details. - Less eager to explain its theme. - More comfortable with unresolved social/emotional consequences. - Willing to make characters contradict themselves. - Willing to let jokes land at inappropriate times. - Specific to one school, one town, one friendship, one night. # Common Telltale LLM Approaches to Writing ## 1. Over-Engineering the Plot LLMs often construct fiction like a clean outline for a screenwriting course: - Inciting incident. - Rising action. - Midpoint reveal. - Dark night of the soul. - Climax. - Resolution. - Final callback. This can be useful for planning, but it can also make a story feel pre-fabricated. Real short fiction often has stranger proportions. A human might spend half a chapter on sneaking across a school corridor if the voice is brilliant, then skip over a plot detail that an outline would treat as essential. For this piece, an over-engineered plot could flatten the fun. The core pleasure is not just “solve the mystery of Ollie’s injury.” It is Nell’s horrified, sarcastic, emotionally tangled experience of one stupid night becoming unmanageably serious. Risk in current plan: - The current plan is quite structurally polished: old code, school lock-in, blood scene, blackmail account, deputy head cover-up, public exposure, final callback. - That structure is satisfying, but it may feel too “designed” unless the actual writing includes enough mess, contradiction, and specificity. ## 2. Excessive Thematic Symmetry LLMs love thematic tidiness: - Blood = periods + injury + shame + truth. - School slogan = hypocrisy. - Final emoji callback = full-circle ending. - Friendship broken by silence = healed by speaking up. These are good thematic connections, but if handled too neatly, they become obvious. Human writing often lets themes emerge sideways. Risk in current plan: - The plan repeatedly states the themes: shame, truth, reputation, blood, student voice. - If the story itself says these things too directly, it may feel like an essay wearing a hoodie. - The final line about the code being repurposed could be too cute if not undercut. Possible humanising approach: - Let the blood motif stay practical and gross, not symbolic in the characters’ mouths. - Don’t have Nell think, “This was about shame and truth.” Have her think, “I had Ollie March’s blood under my thumbnail and a pad stuck to my sleeve.” - Let readers infer the larger point. ## 3. Safe Moral Clarity LLMs often default to a clear moral universe: - Bad student is exposed. - Bad adult is suspended. - Protagonist apologises. - Friend partially forgives. - System issues a bland statement. - Everyone learns something, with caveats. This is emotionally safe. It avoids endorsing bad behaviour. But dark comedy often thrives on moral discomfort. For this piece, a too-safe approach could make the story feel like a school safeguarding case study rather than fiction. A human writer might make the narrator complicit in more uncomfortable ways. Maybe Nell once laughed at Saints Unfiltered. Maybe she submitted something small and thought it didn’t matter. Maybe Tilly is not entirely innocent either; she might have forwarded gossip about someone else before becoming the target. Maybe Ollie is vile but also terrified and pathetic. Risk in current plan: - Nell’s flaw is currently respectable: she failed to defend Tilly. - Tilly is the wronged friend. - Ollie is the blackmailer. - Mr Voss is the institutional villain. - This is clean, maybe too clean. Possible humanising approach: - Give everyone dirt under their fingernails. - Nell’s guilt could be more specific and less noble: she didn’t just stay silent; she sent a laughing emoji to someone, or she watched the video twice, or she was secretly relieved Tilly was the target instead of her. - Tilly could have used the gossip account once against someone she disliked. - Jay might genuinely have stolen something unrelated and be using the chaos as cover. - Bea might be enjoying the drama a bit too much. ## 4. Generic Contemporary YA Voice LLMs often imitate YA through: - Snarky first-person narration. - Pop-cultural adjacent references. - Quippy observations. - Emotionally articulate self-awareness. - Dialogue that sounds like adults performing teenagers. The current plan has a strong voice direction, but there is a danger of “snarky YA narrator template.” Many AI-written teen narrators sound like: > “My anxiety did jazz hands.” or > “Because apparently the universe had chosen violence.” This style can be funny, but it is overfamiliar now. It can feel internetty rather than embodied. Risk in current plan: - Lines like “budget baked potatoes,” “the internet is a cockroach in a hoodie,” “Morally, it’s more like sightseeing,” and “universe has a commitment to structure” are funny in outline form, but if every paragraph has this rhythm, the story may become too polished and quippy. - Teen characters may all sound equally witty. - The humour may become one-liners rather than arising from situation and character. Possible humanising approach: - Vary the comedy. Some jokes should be blunt, stupid, petty, or oddly specific. - Let characters have different kinds of humour: - Nell: dry, internal, observant. - Tilly: sharp, defensive, mean when frightened. - Jay: performs clowning but occasionally says something plain. - Bea: theatrical and melodramatic, but not always funny on purpose. - Let some jokes fail. A human scene often includes awkward jokes no one laughs at. - Use silence. Not every emotional beat needs a quip. ## 5. Over-Explaining Backstory and Stakes LLMs often ensure readers understand everything immediately: - Here is the old code. - Here is why they fell out. - Here is what Saints Unfiltered is. - Here is why Ollie matters. - Here is what Mr Voss represents. A human writer might withhold more and trust the reader to pick things up. They might introduce Saints Unfiltered as a name that makes Nell’s stomach drop before explaining why. They might mention Tilly once blocked her, or that Nell still has Tilly’s hoodie, before telling the full story. Risk in current plan: - Chapter 1 may be overloaded: lock-in premise, hidden phone, old code, Tilly estrangement, backstory, setting, sneaking out. - Chapter 3 may explain the friendship rupture too directly. Possible humanising approach: - Feed backstory through friction. - Let Nell’s reaction reveal history before exposition does. - For example: she sees Tilly’s name and thinks, “For half a second I thought she’d unblocked me to tell me to piss off properly.” - Do not explain the Year 8 period rescue in full immediately. Maybe give a quick visceral memory: Tilly’s grey skirt, Nell’s hoodie round her waist, laughter behind them. Fill it out later. ## 6. Convenient Evidence and Tech LLMs often rely on: - A cached admin page. - A camcorder that happens to be running. - A projector that displays the villain’s message. - A scheduled post that neatly exposes everything. - A phone with all the evidence. These can feel like plot devices rather than organic events. Risk in current plan: - The camcorder in Chapter 7 is especially convenient. - The cached admin page and remote account control may feel slightly thriller-generic. - The projector displaying “READY TO BLEED?” is dramatic but perhaps too polished, like a Netflix teen mystery. - Mr Voss taking Nell’s hidden phone is strong, but the teens then need a believable way to expose him. Possible humanising approach: - Make the evidence messier and partial. - Maybe the drama camcorder didn’t capture the whole event, only audio, or only Nell and Tilly hiding while Mr Voss’s shoes are visible. - Maybe the key evidence is not a cinematic video but a boring email printout, a voice note, a deleted screenshot, or a teacher’s Teams notification. - Maybe the students don’t “hack” anything. They simply know that school passwords are stupid, or Jay saw Mr Voss type one in months ago. - Avoid villainous messages like “READY TO BLEED?” unless the account’s tone has already established that exact melodramatic cruelty. ## 7. Villain with Corporate Jargon A common LLM trope is the institutionally evil adult who says exactly the sort of hypocritical phrases readers are meant to hate: - “Reputational safeguarding.” - “External visibility window.” - “Managed disclosure.” - “Wellbeing optics.” This can be delicious satire, but it can also become too on-the-nose. Human villains rarely speak only in perfect thematic slogans. They also get irritated, mispronounce names, forget passwords, sweat, repeat themselves, and say mundane things. Risk in current plan: - Mr Voss may become a cardboard “bad institution” mouthpiece. - His jargon is funny, but if every line is satirical corporate-speak, he loses reality. - A too-obvious villain reduces tension. Possible humanising approach: - Give Mr Voss a plausible self-justification. He believes teenagers exaggerate, online drama is impossible to police, and one scandal can destroy funding for the school. - Make him tired and frightened too. Not sympathetic exactly, but human. - Let him occasionally sound ordinary: “For God’s sake, Nell, put that down.” - Make some of his points uncomfortably practical: public exposure will hurt students whose secrets are in the files. - Let readers see how institutional cowardice often hides behind plausible concerns. ## 8. Perfectly Timed Public Climax LLMs love a public exposure scene: - Assembly. - Livestream. - Microphone. - Evidence played on screen. - Students speak up one by one. - Villain is disgraced. This is satisfying but familiar, especially in YA. It can feel like a fantasy of justice rather than a real school incident. A human dark comedy might make the exposure more chaotic, compromised, and embarrassing. Risk in current plan: - Chapter 7 currently has a very neat “play the footage at the donor breakfast” climax. - It may feel like the ending of a Disney Channel thriller with added blood. - Students “speaking up one after another” can become sentimental if not handled with acid. Possible humanising approach: - Make the exposure go wrong: - The wrong file plays first: Bea’s toothpaste advert. - The projector sound doesn’t work. - The microphone feedback is unbearable. - A governor thinks it is a drama performance. - Someone’s mum starts shouting about parking before understanding the scandal. - The livestream cuts out after the worst possible frame. - Maybe the truth gets out not through a noble speech but through pettiness, panic, or a technical accident. - Nell’s confession could be small and ugly rather than stirring. - Tilly might be furious that Nell makes it about herself, even while appreciating the support. ## 9. Tidy Emotional Repair LLMs often write apologies as clear moral transactions: - Protagonist admits fault. - Friend accepts partially. - Relationship is healed but “not perfectly.” - This seems nuanced but is still tidy. Human relationships are more jagged. Tilly might accept Nell’s apology, then snap at her ten minutes later. Nell might resent Tilly for needing her. Tilly might use the old code because she has no one else, not because she trusts Nell in a clean way. Risk in current plan: - “Friendship repaired imperfectly” is a good intention, but it may still read as a bow. - Final joke about Tilly actually getting her period may risk reducing the emotional complexity to sitcom symmetry. Possible humanising approach: - Let the friendship end in an uncertain but alive place. - Tilly might say, “I’m still angry,” and Nell might say, “Yeah.” - The final text could be funny but also tender because it shows Tilly is willing to ask again. - Or the final blood emoji could come from someone else, implying the code has spread among girls who need help. - Or Nell sends it to Tilly for the first time, admitting she is the one who needs help. ## 10. Sanitised Teen Behaviour LLMs often keep teenagers oddly responsible: - They want to call an ambulance. - They discuss ethics. - They gather evidence. - They expose wrongdoing. - Their rule-breaking is justified. Real teenagers can be selfish, stupid, hilarious, cruel, and brave in the same minute. In a darkly comic YA story, they should make bad decisions. Risk in current plan: - The teens are rebellious but fundamentally heroic. - Their choices are mostly plot-efficient. - There may not be enough idiotic panic. Possible humanising approach: - They should argue about whether blood stains come out of polyester. - Someone should suggest an obviously terrible plan. - Someone should Google “head wound how much blood normal” and immediately regret it. - They might try to clean something before realising that is legally suspicious. - Nell might be more worried about getting grounded than about institutional corruption for a few shameful seconds. - Jay might take a photo, then delete it, then feel awful. - Bea might insist nobody touch the fake blood because it cost £12 from Amazon and the drama department budget is dead. # Deep Dive Critique of the Current Intention ## What Is Working The current intention has several strong foundations. The **period-code misdirection** is excellent. It is funny, intimate, and specific. It gives the blood emojis a reason to be emojis rather than words. It also gives the opening emotional charge: Tilly would only use that code if things were serious, and Nell still responds despite the friendship rupture. The **school lock-in** is a useful contained setting. It creates a pressure cooker, keeps the cast manageable, and offers natural comic targets: school slogans, staff incompetence, confiscated phones, safeguarding theatre, wellbeing initiatives. The **dark comedy of bureaucracy** fits British YA well. British institutions are often most absurd when trying to appear caring. A school turning a crisis into reputation management is believable and satirically rich. The **friendship rupture** gives the story emotional stakes beyond the mystery. Without it, the plot could become a clever caper. With it, every choice Nell makes has a personal charge: will she stay silent again? The intention to avoid graphic violence and sensationalised assault is wise. The story can be dark without becoming exploitative. The intention to avoid a perfectly neat ending is also good, though the current plan may need to work harder to actually achieve that. ## Where It May Be Too LLM-Smooth The plan is very coherent. Almost too coherent. Everything connects: - The blood emojis link to periods. - Period supplies help with a head wound. - Stage blood complicates real blood. - Gossip account ties to old friendship wound. - Deputy head embodies institutional reputation. - Morning event creates public climax. - Final period joke creates circular ending. That is satisfying in outline form, but if executed too cleanly, it may feel engineered. Human stories often include more misfires and texture. Not every object needs to return; not every symbol needs triple meaning. The story could benefit from one or two elements that are not obviously thematic but feel true: - A terrible school mural. - A student sleeping with an eye mask saying “princess.” - A teacher’s mug that says “Don’t talk to me until I’ve had my coffee” at a crime scene. - The smell of fake blood being oddly sweet. - Someone’s mum calling repeatedly because they forgot to put the bins out. - The fact that emergency foil blankets make everything crinkly and impossible to take seriously. These details may not be “necessary,” but they make fiction breathe. ## The Protagonist Risks Being a Standard Snarky Narrator Nell is currently: - Sharp. - Anxious. - Guilty. - Funny. - Observant. - Morally tested. This is a good protagonist profile, but also a common one. To make her more human, she needs oddities beyond the outline. Questions to deepen her: - What is she needlessly proud of? - What is she petty about? - What is her particular social position at school? - Is she invisible, clever, poor, scholarship, teacher’s pet, chronically late, former try-hard? - Why did she and Tilly become friends in the first place? - Why did their friendship break so badly? - What does Nell want before the text arrives? Sleep? To survive the lock-in? To avoid Tilly? To impress someone? To not check Tilly’s Instagram? - What is her specific cowardice? Conflict avoidance? Fear of being laughed at? Fear of losing her place in the social order? - What kind of jokes does she make when scared — mean, absurd, factual, self-deprecating? She should not be generically “witty.” Her wit should come from a particular worldview. Maybe she is the child of a nurse and has inherited grim practical humour. Maybe she categorises disasters by how hard they are to explain to her mum. Maybe she has a private obsession with school signage. Maybe she constantly imagines what would appear in the school newsletter. ## Tilly Risks Being Mostly a Catalyst Tilly currently sends the text, stands near the blood, confronts Ollie, and forces Nell’s guilt. That is strong, but she risks being more plot device than person. She needs: - Her own humour. - Her own wrongness. - A reason she is difficult to help. - A reason Nell loved being her friend. - Agency in the climax that does not depend on Nell rescuing her. Potential humanising flaws: - Tilly can be cruel when cornered. - She might have deliberately baited Ollie. - She might have lied to Nell in Chapter 2, not about hurting Ollie but about why she came. - She might be furious that Nell is useful now, after being useless when it mattered. - She might not want a big public truth moment because public truth is exactly what harmed her. The code itself could be more complicated. Maybe Tilly used it because she knew Nell would come for a period problem, but might not come if Tilly texted “Ollie’s hurt.” That is manipulative, and human. ## Ollie Risks Being Too Conveniently Bad Ollie as head boy/blackmailer is archetypal. It works, but it could become obvious. He should not simply be “popular boy secretly cruel.” That trope is common. Ways to humanise him without excusing him: - He didn’t start Saints Unfiltered; he inherited it or “moderated” it because he liked control. - He thinks everyone enjoys the account until they are targeted. - He tells himself he is preventing worse rumours by managing what gets posted. - He is under pressure to be perfect and uses the account to keep others lower than him. - He is less mastermind, more coward with access. - When injured, he is genuinely frightened and pathetic. - He might apologise badly: “I didn’t think it was that deep,” which is realistic and enraging. - He might have also protected some secrets, complicating the evidence. A more human Ollie makes the story less moralistic and more uncomfortable. ## Mr Voss Risks Being a Satirical Straw Man The deputy head is a strong comic antagonist, but he needs dimensionality. If he only says jargon, he becomes an LLM-flavoured villain. Better if: - He is not cacklingly evil; he is exhausted, ambitious, and convinced he is making the least bad choice. - He believes the school will be punished if the scandal comes out, and that punishment will hurt students too. - He has been begging the academy trust for mental health funding, and a scandal would end it. - He resents teenagers because their pain arrives as admin. - He is genuinely angry that Ollie is hurt but more angry that it happened tonight. - He helps Ollie physically while hiding evidence emotionally. - He occasionally slips from jargon into blunt menace. This makes him more recognisable. Many institutional failures come from people who think they are being practical. ## The Setting Could Be More Specific “Saint Bartholomew’s Academy” is good, but the school could be sharper. Questions: - Where in Britain? Midlands? North West? Coastal town? Outer London? Welsh border? - Is Saint Bart’s a converted comprehensive now run by an academy trust? - Does it have old buildings and new corporate signage? - What does it smell like? - What are its local status markers? - Are there class tensions between students? - What does the sports hall look like at midnight? The more specific the school, the less AI-generic it feels. Possible details: - New academy logo plastered over old mosaic. - The sixth-form block was promised but never built. - The drama studio used to be a chapel. - The canteen has one mural of diverse smiling children who look nothing like the actual pupils. - The heating is either tropical or absent. - The school’s “Reflection Room” is just isolation with a plant. - The lock-in sponsor sheet has raised £143.27, mostly from grandparents. ## The Charity Lock-In Premise Needs Grounding A midnight school lock-in can feel contrived unless made socially believable. Possible justifications: - It is a sponsored “Sleep Out for Student Minds” event where students sleep in the sports hall to raise money. - It is also being filmed for the academy trust’s wellbeing award submission. - Year 11s are there because prefects and “student leaders” were voluntold. - Phones are confiscated for a “digital detox,” ironic because the scandal is online. - Teachers are present but stretched: one PE teacher, one deputy head, one trainee teacher, maybe a caretaker. Human detail: - Most students only came because attendance earns house points, a non-uniform pass, or a line for college applications. - The provided dinner was jacket potatoes that ran out of cheese. - Everyone is pretending the whole thing is meaningful while secretly trying to survive until breakfast. ## The Gossip Account Needs Freshness Anonymous school gossip accounts are realistic, but familiar in YA. To avoid cliché, it should have a very specific format and culture. Questions: - Is Saints Unfiltered an Instagram account? TikTok? Snapchat? Discord? Airdrop? Anonymous Google Form? - What kind of posts does it make? - Is it funny until it isn’t? - Why do students follow it even if they hate it? - Does Nell follow it? - Did teachers know but dismiss it as “drama”? - Does the account use school branding, saint imagery, confession booth jokes? Potential specific angle: - The account styles itself as a Catholic-style confession page despite Saint Bart’s not being religious anymore. - Posts are called “confessions.” - It uses phrases like “Forgive me, Father, for I have screenshotted.” - Students submit gossip via anonymous form. - It has recurring features: “Sinner of the Week,” “Martyr Monday,” “Canonise or Cancel.” - This could be very funny and dark, and tied to Saint Bartholomew. Need caution: - Don’t make the account too clever or cartoonish. Teen cruelty is often banal. ## The Evidence Trail Needs Less Convenience The current evidence structure is too neat. To humanise, evidence should be messy, ambiguous, incomplete. Instead of: - Perfect camcorder footage showing the accident and Mr Voss taking phone. Consider: - Audio catches Mr Voss saying, “Give me the phone, Oliver,” after the fall. - Video shows only shoes and Tilly’s voice. - A reflection in a drama mirror shows Mr Voss’s tie. - The fake blood bottle has his fingerprint? Too CSI; avoid. - A school iPad auto-uploaded rehearsal footage to the drama department cloud. - Bea had been livestreaming rehearsal notes to her private account by mistake. - Jay saw Mr Voss pocket the phone but nobody believes Jay because Jay lies constantly. - Ollie, concussed, says something incriminating in the office. - Nell notices Mr Voss has changed his shoes by morning, but left the brogues under his desk. A human story may not require courtroom-proof evidence. It only needs enough to break the school’s official version. ## The Public Climax Should Be Less Inspirational The assembly/donor breakfast climax is effective but risks sentimentality. It should be chaotic, funny, and morally compromised. Possible complications: - The students cannot get the main screen to work because the school laptop is updating Windows. - Mr Voss tries to talk over them with a microphone that keeps cutting out. - The local councillor thinks it is a planned student drama piece about online safety and starts clapping. - The footage shows Tilly saying something awful too, making her vulnerable. - Nell’s confession is not eloquent; she says, “I was a massive coward,” then accidentally says “shit” into the microphone. - A parent yells, “Where are the staff?” and another parent says, “I’ve been asking that since Year Seven.” - A teacher tries to form a safeguarding semicircle. - The truth spreads because students start AirDropping/WhatsApping the clip, not because the assembly formally allows it. This would feel more human and more British: justice by shambles. ## The Ending Needs a Bit More Sting The planned final joke — Tilly actually starts her period — is funny and structurally pleasing. It might work. But it risks being too sitcom neat unless shaded with discomfort. Possible alternatives or variations: 1. **Keep it, but make it more awkward.** Tilly texts 🩸🩸🩸 while standing nearby because she does not quite know how to ask Nell directly anymore. That makes the joke emotionally loaded. 2. **Nell sends the emoji.** After everything, Nell texts Tilly 🩸🩸🩸 because she is crying in the toilets and does not want to say so. Tilly comes, complains, and sits beside her. This broadens the code beyond literal blood. 3. **The emoji appears in a new group chat.** Girls from the year start using it as a mutual-aid signal. This might be too neat/empowering, but could be funny if undercut. 4. **It comes from Nell’s mum.** Mum has seen the news, texts blood emojis because she has misunderstood everything, followed by “ARE YOU DEAD OR JUST GROUNDED?” Maybe too comic. 5. **Tilly deletes the emoji thread but does not block Nell.** Quiet, less punchline-driven. The final beat should avoid sounding like a moral slogan. Dark comedy often ends with a laugh that has a bruise under it. # Deep Dive Critique of the Chapter Plan ## Chapter 1 — The Text ### Strengths - Opens exactly on prompt. - Immediate setting and tension. - Hidden phone detail is good. - Old period code gives emotional and comic charge. - Sneaking through the school is atmospheric. ### Risks - Too much exposition packed into the first 1000 words. - The narrator may explain the friendship rupture before readers care. - The lock-in may feel like a contrivance unless made vivid quickly. - The old code might be overexplained rather than revealed through action. ### Humanising Ideas - Start closer and stranger. Nell is not “lying awake”; she is trying not to breathe through her nose because the sports hall smells like Lynx Africa and feet. - She sees the text and reacts physically before explaining. - Maybe she almost ignores it. That hesitation is human and morally interesting. - The old code can be shown through what she grabs: pad, leggings, hoodie, chocolate. The reader infers before full explanation. - Let her resentment toward Tilly flicker: “Typical. Six months of silence and now she wanted Tampax-based special ops.” - Include one embarrassing practical detail: the spare leggings are too small, or have a hole, or say PINK across the bum. - Make the PE teacher asleep in a believable way, not cartoonishly negligent. Maybe she is sitting upright, dead-eyed, listening to a meditation app. ## Chapter 2 — The Wrong Kind of Blood ### Strengths - Great comic reversal. - Period products at a head wound is memorable. - Stage blood plus real blood creates confusion. - Hidden adult shoes are a strong image. ### Risks - “Don’t scream” is a familiar thriller line. - Nell’s joke with the pad could feel too polished if delivered like a sitcom. - Tilly’s explanation of what happened may be too coherent for someone in shock. - The adult shoes clue may feel overly detective-story. ### Humanising Ideas - Tilly might say something more Tilly-specific, like, “Before you do your face, it wasn’t me.” - Nell should not instantly know what to do medically. She might misremember first aid from a Year 9 assembly. - The pad-as-bandage moment can be messy and reluctant: - “Do not use my emergency Always on Ollie March.” - “He’s bleeding from his head.” - “Then tell his head to get its own.” - Tilly may be sick, angry, or obsessing over blood on her nails. - The person entering could do something mundane and chilling: mutter “Oh, for God’s sake,” rather than behave like a shadowy villain. ## Chapter 3 — Digital Detox, My Arse ### Strengths - Nice title. - The confiscated phone premise pays off. - Jay’s introduction can inject energy. - Saints Unfiltered connects personal and plot stakes. ### Risks - Exposition dump about Saints Unfiltered. - Jay with screwdriver/crisps may feel like “quirky sidekick entrance.” - The blood on Jay’s cuff is a classic suspect clue. ### Humanising Ideas - Let Jay be funny but not performatively zany. Maybe he is stealing his own confiscated phone because his little sister is home alone, not just snacks. - Saints Unfiltered can be revealed through Nell’s dread and fragments of posts rather than explanation. - Jay’s blood could have a stupid source — nosebleed, ketchup, fake blood — but he hides it because he has another secret. - The alarm ending is good, but maybe it is not a dramatic alarm; maybe it is the school’s pathetic lockdown chime playing “Greensleeves.” ## Chapter 4 — Everyone Has a Reason ### Strengths - Expands suspects and shows Ollie harmed many people. - Bea can provide tonal contrast. - Tilly’s confrontation with Nell is emotionally important. ### Risks - Suspect tour could feel mechanical. - Everyone having a reason may become too Agatha Christie. - Bea as “drama queen” risks cliché. - Tilly’s line about Nell noticing but not acting is strong but possibly too articulate. ### Humanising Ideas - Rather than interviewing suspects, have students collide because the lock-in has become restless: people are sneaking to toilets, charging phones, finding food. - Bea could subvert the drama stereotype. She is theatrical in presentation but extremely practical about equipment. - Suspicions should arise through accidental discoveries, not formal questioning. - Tilly’s accusation could be uglier and less quotable: “You watched. That was your thing. Watching.” - Nell might snap back unfairly, then regret it. ## Chapter 5 — Saint Ollie’s Gospel of Filth ### Strengths - Title is excellent if the account has saint/confession branding. - The account as a system rather than a single villain is strong. - Mr Voss knowing and delaying action is plausible institutional darkness. ### Risks - Hacking/admin page/cache could feel unrealistic or convenient. - The phrase “Delay intervention until after external visibility window” is almost too perfect. - The projector message is stylised in a way that may feel like teen-drama villainy. ### Humanising Ideas - Make the tech embarrassingly low-tech: - anonymous Google Form, - shared Instagram login, - password in Ollie’s planner, - school email accidentally autofilled, - screenshot folder on a school computer. - The incriminating admin language could be less villainous and more banal: - “Can this wait until after Friday?” - “Please don’t create noise before the visit.” - “We need to be proportionate.” - Replace “READY TO BLEED?” with something more cruelly teenage and specific. Or keep it if the account’s saintly melodrama supports it. - Let the account contain things that implicate Nell, not just other people. ## Chapter 6 — Reputational Safeguarding ### Strengths - Mr Voss’s office as hypocrisy shrine is funny. - Finding Ollie alive shifts the dynamic. - Mr Voss taking Nell’s phone is a strong power move. ### Risks - The office props may be too satirically perfect. - Mr Voss may monologue. - The students may seem too bold in confrontation with a senior teacher. ### Humanising Ideas - Mr Voss should interrupt, redirect, and patronise rather than explain himself. - Ollie should complicate things. Maybe he backs Mr Voss’s version because he is terrified of his own role coming out. - Nell should feel like a child in this room. The power imbalance matters. - Mr Voss taking her hidden phone could be framed as reasonable school discipline, which makes it harder to fight. - There should be a moment where Nell nearly gives up because adult authority still works on her body even when her brain knows better. ## Chapter 7 — Assembly of Lies ### Strengths - Public setting gives stakes. - The irony of “Kindness is Our Culture” is good. - Nell choosing to speak has emotional payoff. ### Risks - Too triumphant. - The camcorder evidence too convenient. - Students speaking up could turn into an inspirational montage. - Nell’s confession may seem designed for character arc completion. ### Humanising Ideas - Make the exposure accidental or botched. - The first thing projected might be ridiculous, undermining the drama. - Nell’s speech should be short and bad. Maybe she does not make a speech; she blurts. - Tilly should have agency and perhaps anger. - Some students might not want exposure and tell Nell to stop. - Some adults might be more concerned with data protection than harm, which is both funny and believable. - The “truth” should not be complete. Enough comes out to rupture the lie. ## Chapter 8 — Aftercare, Academy-Style ### Strengths - “Aftercare” title is excellent dark comedy. - Messy aftermath intention is good. - Foil blanket/baked potato image is funny. - Partial apology is right. - Final blood emoji callback has comic potential. ### Risks - Academy statement satire may be too familiar. - Mr Voss suspended may be too neat. - Saints Unfiltered taken down may feel too easy. - The friendship repair may still feel wrapped up. - Final period joke could be too symmetrical. ### Humanising Ideas - Let consequences be uneven: - Mr Voss is “temporarily absent”
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I intend to write a darkly comic British YA short story in eight short chapters, each around 1000 words, beginning with the original prompt: **The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸** The story will be voice-led rather than machinery-led. The plot will still have a clear mystery shape, but the main pleasure should come from the narrator’s particular, flawed, funny, frightened way of experiencing the night. The tone should feel British, teenage, bleakly comic, and specific: stale sports-hall air, confiscated phones in plastic wallets, cheap squash, wet blazers, academy-trust jargon, teachers pretending the heating works, and students who are hilarious mostly because they are panicking. The story will centre on **Nell Harper**, a Year 11 student at **Saint Bartholomew’s Academy**, a formerly ordinary comprehensive now rebranded into an academy with inspirational wall vinyls and a suspicious number of “wellbeing initiatives.” Nell is clever, observant, conflict-avoidant, and funnier in her head than she is out loud. Her humour is dry and practical rather than endlessly quippy. She is not a perfect moral centre. She has previously chosen social safety over loyalty, and she knows it. The text is from **Tilly Wren**, Nell’s former best friend. The three blood drops are their old private code from Year 8: period disaster, come now, no questions. This is the prompt’s emotional engine. The emojis are intimate, childish, embarrassing, funny, and alarming all at once. Nell thinks she is being summoned to fix a humiliating but manageable problem. Instead, she finds Tilly in the drama studio beside an injured head boy and a great deal of blood — some fake, some real. The story takes place during an overnight school charity event: a sponsored “sleep-out” in the sports hall for student mental health awareness, doubling as publicity for a morning academy-trust donor breakfast. Phones have been confiscated for a “digital detox,” though Nell has kept an old cracked one hidden. This setup should feel absurd but grounded: students have been voluntold to attend, the dinner was bad jacket potatoes, the staff are tired, and the school wants photographs of resilience more than actual resilience. The core mystery will be: **What happened in the drama studio, and why are certain people more concerned with controlling the story than helping the injured boy?** The injured boy, **Ollie March**, is Saint Bart’s golden student: head boy, rugby captain, parent-evening smile, the sort of boy adults describe as “a credit to the school.” He is also involved with an anonymous gossip/confession account called **Saints Unfiltered**, which posts humiliating “confessions” about pupils under mock-religious branding: “Forgive me, Bart, for I have screenshotted.” The account began as a joke, became popular, and then became a machine for cruelty and blackmail. Ollie is not a grand villain mastermind; he is vain, cowardly, clever enough to hurt people, and protected by his usefulness to the school. The adult antagonist is **Mr Voss**, deputy head and guardian of the school’s image. He is not a cartoon villain. He is tired, controlled, ambitious, and convinced that scandal will damage the school’s funding, inspection outcome, and “student wellbeing provision.” He hides behind phrases like “proportionate response” and “proper channels,” but he also slips into ordinary irritation and fear. He does help Ollie physically, but he also pockets evidence and tries to delay disclosure until after the donor event. His failure is not murder; it is institutional cowardice. The central emotional arc is Nell and Tilly’s damaged friendship. Months ago, Saints Unfiltered posted something humiliating about Tilly during a horrible family situation. Nell did not create the post, but she did worse than nothing in Tilly’s eyes: she watched, stayed quiet, and even sent a private message that allowed her to feel kind without risking anything. In the final plan, Nell’s guilt should be sharper and less noble: she may have opened the video more than once, or sent a weak “that’s awful” to someone while doing nothing public. She is not evil, but she was cowardly. This night forces her to decide whether she will be cowardly again. The story will avoid a perfectly clean moral universe. Tilly is not just a victim; she can be sharp, manipulative, and cruel when frightened. She uses the old blood code partly because she trusts Nell and partly because she knows Nell cannot ignore that particular summons. Jay, Bea, and other students may have their own embarrassing or selfish reasons for sneaking around. Ollie is guilty but also scared and injured. Mr Voss is wrong but recognisable. Nell’s eventual decision to speak up is not a flawless heroic speech; it is awkward, angry, and badly timed. The mystery evidence will be deliberately messy rather than conveniently cinematic. There will not be one perfect video that explains everything. Instead, the truth will emerge from fragments: - Tilly’s account of the argument. - Nell’s memory of polished adult shoes in the blood. - Ollie’s missing phone. - A half-recorded rehearsal video or audio clip from Bea’s drama equipment. - Screenshots from Saints Unfiltered. - A dull but damning email or Teams message from Mr Voss saying, essentially, “Can this wait until after Friday?” - Students recognising patterns of adult inaction. The climax will not be an inspirational assembly scene where everyone speaks in turn and justice descends. It will be a shambolic public rupture at the morning donor breakfast. The wrong file may play first. The microphone may squeal. A parent may misunderstand. Mr Voss may try to frame it as a planned student performance. The truth gets out because teenagers are chaotic, petty, digitally nimble, and finally angry enough to stop being managed. The ending will be unresolved but satisfying. Ollie survives. Mr Voss is not dragged away in handcuffs; he is “temporarily absent pending review,” which is funnier and more believable. Saints Unfiltered is taken down, but screenshots linger. The school issues a statement so bland it feels like violence. Nell’s mum is furious and relieved. Nell and Tilly do not return to uncomplicated best friendship, but something real reopens between them. The final beat will return to the blood emoji code in a way that serves the prompt without feeling too neat. Tilly will text 🩸🩸🩸 again from only a few feet away, because after all that she really has started her period and cannot quite bring herself to ask out loud. It should be funny, tender, and slightly bruised: the old code still works, but everything around it has changed. Stylistically, I will aim for: - First-person narration from Nell. - Short, readable chapters with one main scene or turn each. - Dark humour from specificity, not constant one-liners. - Dialogue that allows awkwardness, interruption, silence, and failed jokes. - British school texture: not generic “high school” beats. - Emotional restraint: let guilt and fear show through action and observation rather than speeches. - A balance of farce and real harm. I will avoid: - A murder plot. - Graphic injury detail. - Sexual assault as a twist. - Perfect evidence. - Hacker clichés. - Overly polished teen banter. - Moral lectures about social media. - A tidy institutional victory. - Every symbol being explained in the prose. - Making Nell too noble or Tilly too pure. # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1 — The Text The story opens in the sports hall at Saint Bartholomew’s Academy during the sponsored overnight “sleep-out” for student mental health awareness. It is just after midnight. The hall smells of feet, deodorant, damp coats, and squash. Thirty Year 11s are pretending to be asleep while rustling crisp packets and checking hidden phones. The staff have confiscated everyone’s official phones in labelled plastic wallets for a “digital detox,” but Nell Harper has kept an old cracked phone hidden in her washbag because Saint Bart’s once lost an entire set of mock papers and blamed “humidity.” The text arrives from Tilly Wren: **🩸🩸🩸** Nell’s reaction is immediate and physical. Tilly has not messaged her properly in months. The emojis are their old emergency code from Year 8, when a period leak in maths became a friendship-forging disaster involving Nell’s hoodie, a packet of pads, and Tilly threatening to hex anyone who looked at her skirt. Three blood drops means: come now, bring supplies, no questions. Nell almost ignores it. That hesitation matters. She is tired, angry at being summoned after months of silence, and ashamed of how relieved she is to be needed. Then she starts packing automatically: pads, wipes, spare leggings that are probably too small, a hoodie, half a bar of chocolate. Her body remembers loyalty faster than her pride does. As she sneaks out, the setting comes alive in small bleak details: a PE teacher sitting upright with one eye open, a banner saying **RESILIENCE: LIVE IT**, a student snoring inside a novelty sleeping bag, the locked phone box on the staff table like a shrine to bad judgement. Nell moves through the corridors towards the drama studio, annoyed, worried, and rehearsing sarcastic things she absolutely will not have the courage to say to Tilly. At the drama door, she hears Tilly’s voice from inside. Not crying. Worse: flat and controlled. “Before you do your face,” Tilly says, “it wasn’t me.” Chapter ends as Nell opens the door. ## Chapter 2 — The Wrong Sort Nell enters the drama studio expecting a period crisis. Instead, she finds Tilly standing on the black tarpaulin from the school’s recent *Macbeth* production, hands smeared red, while Ollie March lies on the floor near a stage block with blood on his temple. The room is absurdly theatrical: fake daggers, plastic goblets, a witch’s cauldron made from a recycling bin, and several bottles of fake blood. Some of the red on the floor is glossy and sweet-smelling. Some is not. Nell’s emergency pads suddenly become the most inappropriate and most useful thing she owns. There is a grim, awkward exchange where Tilly objects to using “perfectly good period products” on Ollie March, but Nell presses one to his head wound anyway because he is breathing and bleeding and nobody present has a medical degree unless GCSE Biology counts. Tilly gives her version in fragments, not a neat exposition dump. She came to confront Ollie about **Saints Unfiltered**, the anonymous school confession account that posted humiliating things about her months ago and is apparently about to post something worse. Ollie had his phone. They argued. Someone else came in. The lights went out. Ollie backed up or was shoved — Tilly is not sure — and hit his head on the stage block. When the lights came back, he was down. Nell wants to get an adult. Tilly wants the phone first. Nell is horrified by this, but Tilly snaps that Ollie’s phone is the reason any adult will care. Then the studio lights flicker off again. Someone enters. Nell and Tilly hide behind a rack of costumes. The person mutters something ordinary and irritated — “For God’s sake” — which is somehow worse than a villain line. Nell sees polished black brogues step onto the tarp. The person bends near Ollie. When they leave, Ollie is still there, but his phone is gone. Nell does not see the face. She sees the shoes, the blood smear, and the cuff of an adult’s trousers. Chapter ends with Tilly whispering, “Tell me you saw that.” ## Chapter 3 — Proper Channels Nell and Tilly try to leave the drama block to get help, but Saint Bart’s after midnight is an obstacle course of locked fire doors, motion-sensor lights, and laminated safeguarding notices. Nell’s hidden phone has 6% battery and no signal in the drama corridor. Their official phones are in the staff phone box. The nearest first-aid kit contains plasters, a triangular bandage, and a leaflet about asthma attacks that looks older than most Year 11s. They argue while trying to keep Ollie in the recovery position. Nell says they need an ambulance. Tilly says they need proof. Nell accuses Tilly of caring more about revenge than a bleeding person. Tilly accuses Nell of only discovering principles when nobody is filming. This opens the old wound but does not fully explain it yet. Nell remembers the Saints Unfiltered post about Tilly months ago: the video, the laughter, the way Nell watched it twice because the second time she convinced herself she was checking how bad it was. She sent Tilly a private “you okay?” and did nothing else. Tilly never forgave her, and Nell never quite apologised. They leave Ollie briefly to find an adult or signal. In the corridor they run into **Jay Malik**, who is supposed to be asleep but is instead trying to get into the vending machine area with a screwdriver. He claims this is a political act against overpriced Mini Cheddars. Then he sees Tilly’s hands and Nell’s pad-wrapper trail and stops joking. Jay knows more about Saints Unfiltered than he wants to admit. He was targeted too, after someone filmed him taking leftover food from the canteen. He also saw Ollie earlier arguing with someone near the staff staircase, though he refuses to say who until he knows whether Ollie is dead. Nell says Ollie is not dead. Jay says that is good because murder would really affect the breakfast buffet. A sudden alarm or automated lockdown chime starts from the drama corridor — not dramatic sirens, but the school’s tinny evacuation tone mangling “Greensleeves.” They rush back. Chapter ends when they reach the drama studio and find the tarpaulin empty. Ollie is gone. ## Chapter 4 — Confessions The disappearance of Ollie shifts the night from crisis into nightmare farce. There is a blood smear leading towards the staff staircase, then nothing. Nell is less scared of a murderer than of having to explain to her mum why she lost the head boy. They encounter **Bea Ashcroft**, drama captain, who is wearing pyjama bottoms, a blazer over a hoodie, and an expression of deep personal offence at the misuse of her props. Bea is not just “the drama queen”; she is practical, bossy, and furious because the fake blood cost £12 online and the drama department has to fundraise for batteries. Bea reveals she had set up a camera/tablet earlier to record rehearsal blocking for a devised piece, but she does not know if it was running during the incident. She also knows Ollie had been sniffing around drama storage because Saints Unfiltered had been using theatre photos and old videos for humiliation posts. The chapter widens the world of Saints Unfiltered without becoming a formal suspect parade. Through argument, gossip, and frightened admissions, Nell learns the account has hurt many people: - Jay was mocked for food poverty. - Bea had an old audition video posted. - A quiet student, Maya, was threatened over alleged exam cheating. - Tilly has a new post scheduled about her family situation. - Nell herself once laughed weakly in a group chat rather than challenge anything. The account’s branding is cruelly school-specific: fake saint icons, confession captions, “Sinner of the Week,” “Forgive me, Bart, for I have screenshotted.” It was funny until it wasn’t, except Nell realises it was probably never funny to the person being posted. Tilly and Nell clash hard. Tilly says Nell was always good at looking sorry from a safe distance. Nell snaps back something unfair about Tilly enjoying drama, and instantly regrets it. The fight is ugly, not therapeutic. Bea remembers that the drama tablet auto-uploads to the department cloud when connected to school Wi-Fi. The footage may exist, but they need access to the media room or a staff login. Jay says the media room door lock is broken if you kick it in exactly the place the caretaker told everyone not to kick. Chapter ends with the four of them heading towards the media room, while behind them a staff door clicks shut. ## Chapter 5 — Receipts In the media room, the group tries to access Bea’s uploaded footage. This is not sleek hacking. It involves a filthy keyboard, a forgotten teacher password written under a mouse mat, Jay insisting he is “basically MI5,” and Nell nearly crying because the computer decides to install updates. They find fragments, not the whole truth. The recording captured audio from the drama studio and a slanted view of the floor: - Tilly confronting Ollie. - Ollie laughing and telling her no one believes girls who “make themselves the story.” - The door opening. - A flash of polished shoes. - The lights going out. - A crash. - Mr Voss’s voice, low and furious, saying, “Oliver, give me the phone.” - Tilly saying, “He’s bleeding.” - Mr Voss saying, “Not here. Not tonight.” It does not prove everything, but it proves enough to make Nell’s stomach drop. They also uncover screenshots and messages linked to Saints Unfiltered. The account is less mastermind conspiracy than grubby shared cruelty: anonymous submissions, saved screenshots, students forwarding things, Ollie moderating, others feeding him material. Nell’s own group chat appears in one screenshot. It shows her not creating harm, but orbiting it. She sees her own weak little laughing emoji under someone else’s joke about Tilly. This is worse than being falsely accused. It is being accurately small. The group finds a dull message from Mr Voss to another senior staff member after earlier complaints about the account: **“Can this wait until after the Trust visit? Need to avoid noise before Friday.”** It is not dramatic, but it is damning. They realise the morning donor breakfast begins at 8:30, and Saints Unfiltered has a scheduled post set for around the same time. It will expose Tilly and several others, probably as leverage or entertainment during the school’s big “student voice” showcase. Nell wants to delete everything. Tilly says deleting it will not erase who has already seen it. Bea says they need to take control before Mr Voss does. Jay suggests arson, then admits this is not legally his best idea. Chapter ends when the media room door opens and Mr Voss stands there, calm, tired, and very much wearing different shoes. ## Chapter 6 — Minor Incident Mr Voss does not shout. That is worse. He tells them they are distressed, sleep-deprived, and making serious allegations without context. He says Ollie has had a minor accident and is being looked after. He says online matters must go through proper channels. He says the phrase “duty of care” as if he invented care personally. Nell notices his trouser cuff is damp. His polished brogues are not on his feet anymore. He confiscates Nell’s hidden phone with ordinary teacher authority, which makes her feel suddenly twelve. He warns them that spreading partial information could harm vulnerable students — a point that is not entirely false, which makes it harder. He reminds Jay of his behaviour record. He reminds Bea of her drama scholarship interview. He tells Tilly that family issues are private and should remain private. He says Nell is “usually sensible,” which lands like an insult. They are marched, not quite formally, towards the staff offices. There they see Ollie sitting in Mr Voss’s office with a towel pressed to his head. Ollie is conscious, pale, frightened, and smaller without his audience. He says it was an accident, but he will not look at Tilly. Mr Voss has clearly told him what the story is. Tilly nearly loses control. Nell expects herself to stay quiet again. She can feel the old habit of safety closing over her: let the adult speak, let the embarrassing thing pass, survive the room. Instead, she asks Mr Voss why he took the phone before calling for help. Mr Voss’s mask slips. Only briefly. He says, “Because children with phones can ruin lives before breakfast.” It is not a confession exactly, but it tells them what he fears. He separates them. Nell is sent back to the sports hall under the supervision of a young trainee teacher who clearly knows something is wrong but is terrified of Mr Voss. Tilly is taken elsewhere “to calm down.” Bea and Jay vanish into the flow of waking students. Chapter ends with Nell back among sleeping bags at dawn, phone gone, hands still sticky from someone else’s blood, realising the scheduled post is less than an hour away. ## Chapter 7 — Student Voice Morning at Saint Bart’s is grotesquely cheerful. The donor breakfast begins in the assembly hall: Costco pastries, fruit nobody touches, governors in coats, a local journalist, academy-trust visitors, and a banner reading **KINDNESS IS OUR CULTURE**. Students are herded in as evidence of engagement. Nell has washed her hands four times but still sees red around one thumbnail. Mr Voss begins a speech about resilience, digital citizenship, and student voice. Ollie is absent, officially due to “a minor sporting injury,” which is impressive because the only sport played overnight was competitive denial. Nell feels herself failing again. She has no phone. The adults have the room. The official version is already forming. Then the shambles begins. Bea has not been idle. The drama tablet auto-uploaded the clip, and she has managed to get it onto a USB using the media room computer before Mr Voss arrived. Jay creates a distraction by “accidentally” spilling orange juice across the front row and shouting about an electrical hazard. Tilly appears beside Nell, grey-faced but steady, and says, “If this goes badly, I’m blaming your personality.” They get to the AV desk. It does go badly. The wrong file plays first: Bea’s ancient toothpaste advert, projected thirty feet high in front of governors. Someone starts clapping, thinking it is part of a student presentation. Then the real audio plays over it: Ollie’s voice, Tilly’s voice, the crash, Mr Voss saying, “Not here. Not tonight.” The room changes. Not neatly. Not nobly. Parents murmur. Students start whispering and filming on newly returned phones. Mr Voss tries to stop the playback, calling it a safeguarding breach. That phrase backfires spectacularly. Tilly takes the microphone, but her voice catches. Nell steps in, not with a stirring speech but with an ugly, shaking admission: Saints Unfiltered hurt people, staff knew enough to delay, and she herself saw what happened to Tilly before and did nothing because she did not want the attention moving onto her. She accidentally says “shit” into the microphone. This, more than anything, wakes the room up. Other students do not deliver inspirational testimony one by one. Instead, chaos spreads: someone shouts that they reported the account in October; someone else AirDrops screenshots; a parent asks why no one answered her emails; Jay loudly identifies the account’s shared login; Bea, still projected as a nine-year-old toothpaste enthusiast, yells that this is not the point. Mr Voss tries to regain control, but the story has escaped him. Chapter ends with Nell looking at Tilly, unsure whether she has helped or made everything worse, as the scheduled Saints Unfiltered post fails to go live because someone — maybe Bea, maybe Jay, maybe half the year at once — has changed the password to **VossIsATurnip!** ## Chapter 8 — Aftercare The aftermath is untidy, crinkly, and cold. An ambulance takes Ollie to hospital. He will be fine, though “fine” now includes explaining why his phone contains half the school’s misery. Mr Voss is not arrested in front of everyone. He is “temporarily absent pending review,” which sounds to Nell like he has become a missing parcel. The school sends an email by lunchtime describing the night as “a serious but isolated pastoral incident.” Nell’s mum reads it aloud in reception in a voice that makes two office staff pretend to need photocopying. Parents arrive. Students cry, gossip, exaggerate, retract, and send screenshots. Saints Unfiltered disappears, then half-reappears in saved images, because the internet is not a bin; it is a landfill with Wi-Fi. The academy trust promises an independent review. Nobody knows what that means, but it sounds expensive. Nell’s mum is furious: about the hidden phone, the blood, the fact Nell did not call her, and the phrase “minor incident,” which she repeats as if she would like to fight it in a car park. She also hugs Nell hard enough to hurt. Nell and Tilly end up outside by the bins, wrapped in foil blankets. This should not become a perfect reconciliation. Tilly is still angry. Nell apologises properly, without asking to be forgiven. She admits not just that she was silent, but that silence suited her. Tilly says, “Yeah. It did.” Then, after a while, she says, “You were less useless tonight.” It is not absolution. It is something. They laugh, briefly, about the pad used on Ollie’s head wound. Tilly says if Saint Bart’s ever does a period-product fundraiser now, she is demanding keynote speaker status. Nell says the school will call it “Absorbency and Resilience Week.” Tilly nearly smiles. The final beat returns to the prompt’s code. Nell’s recovered phone buzzes. Tilly is sitting three feet away from her, staring straight ahead. **🩸🩸🩸** Nell looks up, alarmed despite everything. Tilly says, without looking at her, that after all that, she has actually started her period and has bled through the spare leggings during a police statement, because apparently her uterus believes in comic timing. For a second, Nell does not know whether to laugh or cry. Then she reaches into her bag. Of course she still has supplies. She packed for blood. She just had not known which sort.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Nell Harper Nell Harper is sixteen, Year 11, and lives in a small terrace on the edge of town with her mum, a part-time NHS healthcare assistant, and her younger brother Finn, who is nine and regards Nell’s GCSE revision timetable as a personal insult. Her dad exists in the vague administrative sense — birthday texts, occasional bank transfers, a Facebook profile picture with a woman Nell calls “Pilates Sandra” — but he is not emotionally central to her life. Nell has grown up in a house where things are usually manageable but tight: packed lunches made from whatever is left, school trips paid for in instalments, shoes expected to last “until they properly die.” She is clever, observant, and conflict-avoidant. Teachers describe her as “sensible,” which she has come to realise means “unlikely to make extra work for us.” She is good at English and Biology, bad at PE, and catastrophically bad at saying the brave thing at the right time. In her own head she is sharp, articulate, scathing. Out loud, under pressure, she often says “yeah, no, sorry” and then hates herself for three days. Nell’s defining flaw is not ignorance. She usually knows when something is wrong. Her flaw is that she can persuade herself that doing nothing is the same as not doing harm. She has a strong sense of justice when the stakes are theoretical, but in actual social danger she freezes. When Saints Unfiltered humiliated Tilly, Nell did not join the attack, but she watched, lingered, sent weak private sympathy, and stayed publicly silent. She has spent months trying to call that “not making it worse.” Deep down she knows better. She uses humour as a pressure valve. Her humour is dry, practical, slightly bleak, and often aimed at institutions, herself, or physical inconvenience. She is not constantly quippy. Sometimes she thinks of a perfect comeback twelve minutes too late and stores it bitterly like a Victorian orphan saving coal. She notices signs, slogans, smells, badly designed systems. Saint Bart’s enrages her because it is full of language pretending to be care. Idiosyncrasies: - She keeps old phones, chargers, safety pins, plasters, and sanitary pads in every bag because her mum raised her to believe “someone always needs something.” - She mentally rewrites school posters. **ACHIEVE YOUR POTENTIAL** becomes **SURVIVE UNTIL HALF THREE**. - She counts exits when anxious, then pretends she does not. - She hates people touching her neck or reading over her shoulder. - She cannot sleep in rooms where other people are asleep; the breathing noises make her feel trapped. - She is secretly proud of having good handwriting. - She has a habit of picking dried glue or labels off things while thinking. - She lies badly to adults but very well by omission. - She always says she does not care about being liked, then checks if people laughed at her joke. Physically, Nell is slight, narrow-shouldered, and looks younger than she wants to, which annoys her intensely. She has brown hair cut just below her chin because she once tried to give herself “layers” and her mum had to rescue it at 10 p.m. Her fringe is usually clipped back with a plain black grip that slides down her face when she is stressed. She wears her blazer sleeves over her hands, not because it is cute but because the cuffs are fraying and she likes having something to pull at. When nervous, she goes very still, eyes moving more than her body. When angry, she speaks too quickly and then apologises before anyone has responded. Her core motive at the beginning is to get through the lock-in without drama, avoid Tilly, and preserve whatever fragile social safety she has built since their friendship collapsed. When the text arrives, a deeper motive takes over: Tilly needs her, and Nell cannot bear to fail the exact same test twice. Her allegiance is conflicted at first — to safety, to the rules, to not being noticed — but shifts towards Tilly and the truth. She does not become fearless. She simply reaches a point where silence feels more dangerous than speaking. Existing relationships: - **Tilly:** former best friend, still emotionally central. Nell feels guilt, resentment, longing, and fear around her. - **Mum:** loving but strained; Nell hates worrying her and hates being treated like a child. - **Jay:** knows him as a funny troublemaker; underestimates him. - **Bea:** slightly intimidated by Bea’s certainty; finds her ridiculous but useful. - **Ollie:** does not know him well personally; has accepted the school’s golden-boy mythology more than she wants to admit. - **Mr Voss:** sees him as the kind of adult who can ruin your day with calm sentences. Flavour dialogue / internal monologue: - “The thing about Saint Bart’s is they never solved a problem if they could laminate it instead.” - “I didn’t ignore Tilly. That was the worst bit. I did something just close enough to kindness that I could hide behind it.” - “No, obviously I didn’t bring medical equipment. I brought pads, leggings and a KitKat. I was prepared for biology, not manslaughter.” - “I had a whole speech ready in my head, which was unfortunate because my mouth had gone with ‘um’ as its main strategy.” # Tilly Wren Tilly Wren is sixteen, also Year 11, and lives with her mum and younger half-sister in a flat above a parade of shops: nail bar, betting shop, charity shop, empty unit, chicken place that changes names every six months but keeps the same chips. Her family situation is complicated and more public than she wants. Her parents separated badly; there have been court dates, money arguments, and social services-adjacent meetings that adults call “support” while making everything feel contaminated. Tilly has developed an intense hatred of pity. She used to be Nell’s best friend in the all-consuming way girls can be at thirteen: shared hoodies, private codes, elaborate running jokes, dramatic voice notes, paired seating plans negotiated like treaties. Tilly was the louder one, the social engine, the person who could walk into a room and decide where the oxygen went. Nell was her witness, translator, co-conspirator. Their friendship ended after Saints Unfiltered posted a humiliating video of Tilly crying behind the science block after a family court meeting. What broke Tilly was not just the post; it was looking around and realising who was watching. Tilly is funny, sharp, and performatively fearless. She has a gift for making people laugh and then cutting them if they laugh at the wrong thing. She is proud, defensive, and allergic to being managed. She would rather be thought difficult than vulnerable. Her anger is both armour and weapon. She can be manipulative when scared — using the 🩸🩸🩸 code on Nell is partly trust, partly strategy. She knows Nell will come for that. She is not above pressing the bruise. She is also deeply loyal when loyalty is returned. If Tilly loves someone, she will bring them chips, lie to cover them, remember their mum’s shift patterns, and insult them in exactly the way that makes them feel known. She hates grand apologies but notices small practical reparations. She does not forgive quickly because forgiveness, to her, often feels like giving someone permission to say it was never that bad. Idiosyncrasies: - She always carries lip balm but never a working pen. - She drinks tea that is 80% milk and gets defensive about it. - She gives inanimate objects legal threats: “This zip is on its final warning.” - She screenshots everything, partly from paranoia, partly from experience. - When lying, she becomes over-specific. - When genuinely upset, she goes eerily calm. - She has a habit of saying “not to be dramatic” immediately before saying something dramatic and accurate. - She hates the word “resilience” because adults use it when they mean “put up with it.” - She is weirdly good at folding fitted sheets because her nan taught her. - She cannot bear people whispering near her; she assumes she is the subject. Physically, Tilly is compact, quick-moving, and expressive. She has dyed dark-red hair that has faded unevenly at the ends, giving her an accidental fox-like look she pretends was intentional. She wears chunky hoops, chipped black nail polish, and school uniform customised just enough to annoy staff but not enough to trigger isolation: skirt rolled once, tie too loose, blazer with badges hidden inside the lapel. Her face is mobile — eyebrows doing half the conversation — but when she is frightened she locks it down completely. She stands with her weight forward, like she is about to argue, run, or both. Her motive at the beginning is to stop Ollie and Saints Unfiltered from posting more of her private life. She wants evidence, control, and revenge, not in an abstract villainous way but because she is sick of being the thing that happens to other people online. She also wants Nell there, though she would rather chew glass than admit it plainly. Her allegiance is to herself first, then to anyone else harmed by the account, then cautiously, painfully, back towards Nell. Existing relationships: - **Nell:** former best friend. Tilly still knows exactly how to reach her and hates that she still wants to. - **Ollie:** despises him; sees him as a coward hiding behind charm. - **Mr Voss:** does not trust him; has likely been patronised by him before. - **Jay:** banter-based alliance; she finds him annoying but useful. - **Bea:** respects Bea’s competence but mocks her theatre seriousness. - **Mum:** loves her fiercely, but resents the chaos that has made her life gossip material. Flavour dialogue / internal monologue: - “I didn’t say I had a good plan. I said I had a plan. Those are different genres.” - “Don’t do your concerned face.
