It makes you look like a spaniel in a charity advert.” - “Adults love saying ‘proper channels’ like the channel isn’t a ditch with a clipboard in it.” - “I used the code because I knew you’d come. Sorry if that’s emotionally complicated for you. It was also efficient.” # Jay Malik Jay Malik is sixteen, in Year 11, and lives on the estate near the dual carriageway with his mum, his older cousin Imran who is “staying temporarily” and has been there for eleven months, and his two younger sisters. His mum works shifts at a care home and sleeps like someone falling down a lift shaft. Jay has grown up with more responsibility than he admits. He gets his sisters to school, knows which shops reduce bread at what time, and can stretch £3.40 into dinner if nobody is fussy. At school, Jay is known as a clown, nuisance, and “bright but unfocused.” This is not inaccurate, but it is incomplete. He jokes because it gives him control over how people look at him. If he makes himself the joke first, there is less left for other people to take. He has detentions like other people have hobbies, mostly for talking, lateness, uniform, and “attitude,” which often means he asked a fair question in the wrong tone. Jay was targeted by Saints Unfiltered after someone filmed him taking leftover food from the canteen bins/serving area to bring home. The account framed it as theft and made him a meme. He leaned into it publicly — “yeah, free school meals, innit, very Ocean’s Eleven” — but it burned him badly. Since then, his humour has acquired an edge. He wants Ollie exposed, but he is also scared of being exposed again. He is resourceful in a grubby, practical way. He knows which locks are weak, which teachers leave passwords on sticky notes, which corridors have cameras that do not work, which vending machine accepts a bent coin if you swear at it first. He is not a hacker genius. He is observant because institutions are full of gaps, and he has spent his life slipping through them. Idiosyncrasies: - He gives fake formal titles to petty crimes: “Operation Biscuit Retrieval.” - He always has a snack, but never admits he is hungry. - He says “respectfully” before being deeply disrespectful. - He doodles elaborate monsters in the margins of worksheets. - He knows every teacher’s car and judges them for it. - He cannot sit on a chair properly; he perches, sprawls, tips back, or half-stands. - He deflects concern with jokes so quickly people often let him. - He is strangely gentle with younger kids and animals. - He hates being called “cheeky.” - He gets nervous when adults speak softly rather than shouting. Physically, Jay is wiry, restless, and all elbows. He has dark curly hair that never lies flat, brown skin, and a grin he deploys like a smoke bomb. His uniform is always technically wrong: trainers instead of shoes, top button undone, jumper with a hole in the cuff, tie at a length suggesting protest. He moves quickly and quietly when he wants to, but in public he performs looseness — slouching, leaning, bouncing on his toes. His face changes fast: grin, blankness, calculation, grin again. His motive is partly justice, partly revenge, partly self-preservation. He wants Saints Unfiltered stopped because it hurt him and because it could hurt his sisters one day. He also wants not to become the obvious scapegoat. He knows that if something goes missing or breaks, eyes turn to him first. His allegiance is initially to himself and his family, then to the improvised student group once he realises they are all trapped in the same machine. Existing relationships: - **Nell:** sees her as clever but timid; needles her, but respects her when she acts. - **Tilly:** banters with her; recognises her anger as similar to his own. - **Bea:** enjoys irritating her; also trusts her organisational skills. - **Ollie:** hates him with the particular hatred reserved for someone who made poverty into content. - **Mr Voss:** fears him more than he admits because Voss has power over references, behaviour reports, and “future prospects.” - **Family:** fiercely protective, especially of his sisters. Flavour dialogue / internal monologue: - “Respectfully, sir, if the safeguarding cupboard is locked during an emergency, that’s not safeguarding, that’s furniture.” - “I wasn’t breaking in. I was testing the school’s commitment to access.” - “Everyone’s got a plan until the computer says it’s updating for forty-seven minutes.” - “Ollie March calling me a thief was rich. Man stole oxygen every time he spoke in assembly.” # Bea Ashcroft Bea Ashcroft is sixteen, Year 11, drama captain, school council veteran, and the sort of person adults call “impressive” when they mean “slightly terrifying but useful.” She lives in a semi-detached house with her dad, who sells insurance and has never knowingly under-reacted to a thermostat, and her stepmum, who runs local amateur musical productions and says things like “project from the diaphragm” during arguments. Bea has been in dance classes, youth theatre, and school productions since she was small, and she has the organisational intensity of someone who has spent years around chaotic adults in rehearsal rooms. Bea presents as theatrical, but she is not merely flamboyant comic relief. She is practical, controlling, and deeply serious about competence. She knows where the extension leads are, which prop knives are safe, who has signed consent forms, and exactly how little money the drama department has. She takes offence when people misuse equipment because she knows how hard it is to get anything replaced. Her relationship with Saints Unfiltered is partly comic, partly painful. The account posted an old video of her auditioning for a toothpaste advert when she was nine, complete with a jingle and an alarming amount of confidence. Bea pretended to own it, reposted a still with a joke, and made everyone think she was unbothered. In truth, she was humiliated — not because the video was terrible, but because it showed a younger version of herself before she learned to manage how she was seen. Bea’s deepest fear is not embarrassment; it is loss of control over the performance. Idiosyncrasies: - She carries a clipboard even when no clipboard is needed. - She calls school equipment by dramatic names: the lighting desk is “Geraldine.” - She owns a tiny emergency sewing kit and uses it with battlefield seriousness. - She says “darling” only when furious. - She has strong opinions about fonts on posters. - She cannot whisper properly; her whisper reaches the back row. - She alphabetises scripts but loses her own house keys weekly. - She is weirdly calm during practical crises and dramatic during minor inconveniences. - She has never forgiven the English department for borrowing chairs and returning three. - She labels chargers with nail varnish. Physically, Bea is tall, long-limbed, and moves as though aware of sightlines. She has pale skin, a sharp chin, and hair usually scraped into a bun so tight it suggests personal discipline and mild pain. Her uniform is immaculate but accessorised with something slightly theatrical: a vintage brooch, dramatic scarf, eyeliner too precise for 8:30 a.m. She gestures with her whole hand, not just fingers, and has a habit of turning her head before speaking as if finding her light. When stressed, she becomes brisk rather than shaky. Her motive is to stop Saints Unfiltered because it violates her sense of justice, privacy, and aesthetic standards. She also wants to protect the drama department from being implicated in a scandal involving fake blood and head injuries, because she knows the arts will somehow get blamed. Bea is loyal to competence first: whoever is being useful gets her support. Over the night, she becomes loyal to Nell and Tilly because they keep going despite being messy amateurs. Existing relationships: - **Nell:** sees potential in her but is impatient with her hesitation. - **Tilly:** admires Tilly’s bite but distrusts her impulsiveness. - **Jay:** finds him exasperating; secretly appreciates his nerve. - **Ollie:** dislikes him for using people’s images as entertainment. - **Mr Voss:** has worked with him on school events and knows his polished public self; recognises the danger in his calmness. - **Drama department:** treats it like a fragile kingdom under siege. Flavour dialogue / internal monologue: - “If anyone gets real blood on my Macbeth tarp, I am becoming a problem for this institution.” - “No, Jay, kicking a door is not a security strategy. It is, at best, percussion.” - “I don’t mind humiliation. I mind poor framing.” - “Darling, if this school cared about student voice, the microphones would work.” # Ollie March Ollie March is sixteen, Year 11, head boy, rugby captain, predicted straight 8s and 9s, and the face Saint Bart’s likes to put in brochures because he looks clean, confident, and reassuringly expensive despite not actually being posh. He lives in a newer estate with his mum, a primary school deputy head, and his stepdad, who runs a small building firm and talks about “graft” a lot. Ollie is used to adults liking him. He has learned how to smile at them, how to lower his voice, how to call female teachers “Miss” with just the right level of respect, how to look sorry without surrendering anything. He is not stupid, and he is not simply evil. He is charming, socially intelligent, vain, competitive, and cowardly. He likes being central. Saints Unfiltered began before his full involvement — maybe as a stupid confession page run by several students — but Ollie became one of its main handlers because it gave him invisible power. He could decide what got posted, what got buried, who got warned, who got mocked. He told himself everyone followed it, everyone laughed, everyone submitted things, so he was not uniquely guilty. This is his favourite moral hiding place: if everyone is doing it, nobody is. Ollie’s cruelty is often plausible-deniable. He rarely says “I will ruin you.” He says, “Careful, that would be embarrassing if people saw it.” He frames threats as banter. He has a gift for identifying what someone is ashamed of and brushing against it lightly enough that outsiders hear nothing. He likes control more than chaos, which is why the night scares him. Once blood is real, not digital, he is out of his depth. Idiosyncrasies: - He checks his reflection in dark windows but pretends he is looking outside. - He uses people’s full names when he wants to sound reasonable. - He says “mate” to boys he is threatening and “come on” to girls he is patronising. - He keeps his school planner immaculate because teachers notice. - He has a habit of rolling his shoulders like he is about to take a penalty kick. - He chews mint gum before assemblies. - He is obsessed with not looking rattled. - He calls online cruelty “content” or “drama” because those words make it seem weightless. - He remembers embarrassing details about people with frightening accuracy. - He hates being laughed at by a group. Physically, Ollie is broad-shouldered, athletic, and irritatingly symmetrical. He has sandy-brown hair kept deliberately casual, clear skin, and the sort of posture that makes teachers think “leadership” before he has spoken. His uniform is neat in a way that looks effortless: tie correct, blazer clean, shirt sleeves rolled exactly once in warm rooms. He moves comfortably through space as if expecting doors, conversations, and people to open for him. When injured, that physical confidence collapses; he becomes pale, blinking, boyish, and suddenly much less mythic. His motive at the beginning is to keep control of Saints Unfiltered, prevent Tilly from exposing his involvement, and maintain his golden image through the donor breakfast. After the accident, his motive shifts to self-preservation. He is frightened of consequences, of his parents, of losing head boy status, of becoming the joke. His allegiance is to himself, but he leans towards Mr Voss because Voss offers an official story he can hide inside. Existing relationships: - **Nell:** barely registers her except as Tilly’s former friend; underestimates her. - **Tilly:** sees her as dangerous because she refuses to be quietly ashamed. - **Jay:** has used Jay’s poverty as content; considers him trouble. - **Bea:** finds her annoying because she cannot be charmed easily. - **Mr Voss:** mutually useful relationship; Voss likes his poster-boy value, Ollie likes adult protection. - **Adults generally:** knows how to perform goodness for them. Flavour dialogue / internal monologue: - “You’re making this bigger than it is, Tilly. That’s kind of your thing, isn’t it?” - “It’s a confession page. People send stuff in. Don’t act like I invented gossip.” - “Come on, Nell. You’re sensible. You know how this looks.” - “Nobody was meant to get hurt. It was just posts. It was just people talking.” # Mr Daniel Voss Mr Daniel Voss is in his late thirties or early forties, deputy head at Saint Bartholomew’s Academy, responsible for behaviour, safeguarding, public events, and the sort of initiatives that come with branded lanyards. He is not the headteacher, but he is the person who makes the school feel governed. He came into education, at least originally, wanting order to mean safety. Over the years, order has become the point. He is ambitious, tired, and deeply invested in the story of Saint Bart’s as an improving school. He lives alone in a flat near the station, though students know almost nothing about him except that he drives a very clean grey car and drinks peppermint tea from a metal flask. He has no children, which students treat as evidence and staff treat as irrelevant. He is good at his job in measurable ways: attendance has improved, exclusions look cleaner on spreadsheets, parents get prompt emails, governors like him. He is also very good at turning messy human distress into language that can be filed. Mr Voss is dangerous because he is plausible. He does not think of himself as someone who covers things up. He thinks of himself as someone who prevents panic, avoids reputational damage, and protects the school’s broader interests. He believes teenagers exaggerate online conflict, that social media scandals spread faster than facts, and that a single incident can derail funding for genuinely useful support. Some of this is not entirely wrong. His failure is that he repeatedly chooses institutional control over vulnerable pupils, and calls it care. On the night of the story, he enters the drama studio after Tilly and Ollie’s confrontation, sees Ollie injured, and immediately understands the danger: blood, phone, gossip account, donor breakfast, Trust visit, his own delayed response to earlier complaints. He helps move Ollie and gets him basic care, but he also takes the phone and tries to keep the incident contained until morning. His first instinct is not evil. It is management. That is the horror. Idiosyncrasies: - He clicks his pen twice before delivering bad news. - He never runs in corridors; he walks quickly with controlled urgency. - He uses pupils’ names too often in conversation as a dominance tactic disguised as warmth. - He says “I hear you” when he wants someone to stop talking. - His shoes are always polished. - He cannot stand untucked shirts or unlabelled cables. - He has a drawer of confiscated jewellery in tiny envelopes. - He speaks more softly when angry. - He treats the word “context” as a shield. - He corrects noticeboard alignment while discussing serious matters. Physically, Mr Voss is medium height, neat, and compact. He has close-cropped dark hair beginning to grey, rimless or thin-framed glasses, and a face that can arrange itself into professional concern at speed. His suits are not expensive but are immaculate. He carries himself with contained authority: shoulders still, hands clasped behind his back, head slightly tilted as if evaluating evidence. He rarely touches students or objects unnecessarily. When stressed, he smooths his tie and checks his cuffs. His motive is to protect the school’s reputation, the donor event, and his own position. He would describe this as protecting the school community. He is also motivated by fear: fear of scandal, fear of losing control, fear that months of image-management will collapse because children with phones can make adults answer questions before the adults have agreed on answers. His allegiance is to Saint Bart’s as an institution, then to himself, then abstractly to students as a group — rarely to any individual student in front of him. Existing relationships: - **Nell:** sees her as sensible, therefore useful and containable. - **Tilly:** sees her as volatile and complicated; possibly already labelled as “high need” or “emotionally dysregulated” in pastoral language. - **Jay:** sees him as a behaviour concern; underestimates his intelligence. - **Bea:** values her for events and student leadership; expects compliance. - **Ollie:** sees him as a success story worth protecting, until he becomes a liability. - **Other staff:** some admire him, some fear him, some are too tired to challenge him. Flavour dialogue / internal monologue: - “Nell, I understand that you feel something serious has happened. Feeling and knowing are different things.” - “We are not going to turn an accident into a spectacle because a group of tired children have half a video.” - “Proper channels exist to protect people from exactly this sort of panic.” - “Children with phones can ruin lives before breakfast.” # Mrs Harper Mrs Harper, first name **Louise**, is Nell’s mum. She is in her late thirties or early forties and works as a healthcare assistant at the local hospital, mostly on long shifts that leave her feet swollen and her patience dependent on tea. She is loving, practical, and permanently tired in a way that has become part of the furniture of Nell’s life. She is not a saintly struggling mother stereotype; she can be funny, irritable, blunt, and unfair when frightened. Louise raised Nell to be prepared: carry pads, carry tissues, keep emergency change, text when you arrive, never assume someone else has sorted it. She has a low tolerance for institutions using soft language to cover bad practice, partly because she sees it at work. She believes in rules when rules keep people safe, and she believes in breaking them when they become stupid, though she would prefer Nell not discover this through potential crime scenes. She is proud of Nell’s cleverness but worries about her tendency to swallow feelings until they become headaches. She did not fully understand what happened between Nell and Tilly, only that Nell became quieter and more brittle. Louise liked Tilly, though she pretended not to notice how often Tilly came round when things were rough at home. Idiosyncrasies: - She calls everyone “love” except when truly angry. - She leaves notes on the fridge that begin polite and end threatening: “Milk in fridge. Do not drink all of it, Finn, I am not a cow.” - She falls asleep during television and then insists she was watching. - She carries hospital-grade hand cream and offers it like emotional support. - She can identify illness severity from the sound of someone saying “Mum.” - She has strong views about cheap shoes. - She texts in full sentences with punctuation, which Nell finds ominous. - She says “I’m not being funny” when she is about to be very funny and very cross. Physically, Louise is short, sturdy, and brisk, with dark-blonde hair usually twisted into a claw clip and a face that looks softer when she is tired. She wears trainers even when not at work because her feet have unionised. Her hands are dry from washing them constantly. She has a way of looking at Nell that makes lying feel not immoral but logistically impossible. When anxious, she tidies aggressively. Her motive in the story is simple and fierce: keep Nell safe. She is not part of the night’s central action, but her presence matters because Nell measures danger partly by “how impossible this will be to explain to Mum.” In the aftermath, Louise is furious not because Nell broke rules in the abstract, but because Nell was frightened and bleeding-adjacent and did not call her. Her allegiance is to Nell first, truth second, school reputation nowhere. Existing relationships: - **Nell:** loving, close, often tense because Nell hides things to avoid adding stress. - **Tilly:** fond of her in a wary way; knows more about Tilly’s home situation than Nell thinks. - **Saint Bart’s:** distrusts its polished emails and believes half its initiatives are “posters with funding.” - **Mr Voss:** the kind of calm man she instinctively wants to argue with. Flavour dialogue / internal monologue: - “You can tell Mr Voss from me that ‘minor incident’ is what I call burning toast, not my daughter coming home with blood on her sleeves.” - “I’m not angry because you helped her. I’m angry because you thought helping her meant you had to be alone.” - “If that school sends me one more email with the word robust in it, I’m going down there with robust shoes.” - “Nell, love, look at me. Are you hurt, in trouble, or both?” # Maya Singh Maya Singh is sixteen, Year 11, quiet but not meek, and one of the students harmed by Saints Unfiltered. She is not part of the main midnight group for the whole story, but she matters as a reminder that the account’s damage extends beyond Nell and Tilly. Maya lives with her parents and grandmother, who all take school seriously in slightly different ways. She is under intense academic pressure, partly external, partly self-imposed, and is known for being top set, tidy notes, and always having spare highlighters. Saints Unfiltered targeted Maya with a rumour that she cheated on a mock exam. The rumour was false or at least distorted: she had seen a leaked question after someone sent it to a group chat she did not ask to be in, panicked, and said nothing. The account framed her as a cheat, which threatened the identity she had built around being quietly excellent. Teachers treated it as “online drama,” but Maya felt watched in every classroom afterwards. Maya is controlled, precise, and more angry than she appears. She does not like public scenes. She may not join the central group’s chaotic investigation, but in the climax she can provide one of the crucial screenshots or speak up sharply from the audience. Her contribution should be small but grounded: not an inspirational monologue, but a sentence that cuts. Idiosyncrasies: - She colour-codes everything, including emotions in her revision planner. - She always has hand sanitiser but hates being asked for it. - She corrects people’s grammar under her breath. - She has a very dry sense of humour that many people miss. - She eats the same lunch every day for comfort. - She hates being called “model student.” - She can hold eye contact longer than most adults. - When stressed, she becomes extremely polite. Physically, Maya is petite, neat, and composed, with long dark hair usually in a low plait and glasses she pushes up with one finger when irritated. Her uniform is correct, not because she loves rules but because she cannot be bothered to give teachers easy reasons. She moves quietly and deliberately. When she speaks, people sometimes lean in because she does not raise her voice. Her motive is to protect her future and stop the account without becoming its next spectacle. Her allegiance is initially to self-preservation. In the morning, when the truth is already breaking open, she chooses to add evidence because silence has not protected her either. Existing relationships: - **Nell:** knows her academically; perhaps sat near her in Biology; sees Nell as decent but passive. - **Tilly:** sympathetic but cautious; Tilly’s loudness unsettles her. - **Ollie:** distrusts him; suspects he helped spread the cheating rumour. - **Mr Voss:** has already reported concerns and been brushed off. - **Jay:** may have shared detention or banter; respects his nerve from a distance. Flavour dialogue / internal monologue: - “I reported it on the form you told us to use. Twice. I got an automated reply and a poster about kindness.” - “I’m not crying. My eyes are just making a poor strategic decision.” - “If I wanted to cheat, I’d have done it competently.” - “Please don’t make me inspirational. I’m extremely annoyed, not brave.”

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1 — The Text The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸 For a second, I thought I’d hallucinated it. This was possible. Saint Bartholomew’s Academy sports hall at midnight was the sort of place where the human brain started inventing threats just to keep itself entertained. The air smelled of feet, damp coats, squash, and the concentrated panic of thirty Year 11s pretending to be asleep for charity. Someone near the climbing ropes farted and immediately blamed their sleeping bag. “Wasn’t me,” whispered Callum Teague, which meant it absolutely was. A teacher made a noise from the trestle table by the fire exit. Not a disciplinary noise. More of a dying-seal noise. Miss Pryce from PE was sitting upright in a plastic chair, arms folded, one eye half open, as if sheer hatred was keeping her conscious. Beside her sat the sacred phone box: thirty-two labelled plastic wallets full of confiscated mobiles, because tonight was not only a sponsored sleep-out for student mental health awareness but also a “digital detox experience.” Saint Bart’s loved an experience. It meant they didn’t have to fix anything. My actual phone was in the box, zipped into a wallet with HARPER, NELL written on it in Mr Voss’s neat deputy-head handwriting. My emergency phone — Mum’s old cracked Samsung, which only charged at a certain angle and had the battery life of a mayfly — was hidden inside my washbag under a packet of pads and one humiliatingly fluffy sock. That was the phone now glowing in my palm. Tilly Wren. 🩸🩸🩸 I stared until the blood drops blurred. Tilly hadn’t texted me in months. Not properly. Not unless you counted one message in September saying “you left your bio revision guide in art” with no kiss, no insult, no follow-up voice note of her singing something horrible from TikTok. Before that, she used to send me thoughts as they arrived, unfiltered and usually libellous. miss says mitochondria is powerhouse of cell but what about rage if i die in french tell my mum i hated the verb table emergency: do u reckon ducks know they’re wet Then nothing. And now three blood drops. My stomach did a small, stupid flip. The code was from Year 8. Maths, third period, Mr Hesketh doing fractions with the joyless confidence of a man who ironed socks. Tilly had stood up and there it was on the back of her grey skirt: a dark red patch shaped vaguely like Australia. Three boys had made noises like they’d discovered comedy. Tilly had gone white. I had taken off my hoodie, tied it round her waist, and told Aaron Bell if he looked again I’d tell everyone he cried during the fire drill. After that, 🩸🩸🩸 meant: period disaster, come now, bring supplies, no questions, and if anyone laughs we bury them. It was a sacred system. Stupid, but sacred. I looked across the sports hall. Tilly was not in her sleeping bag. Of course she wasn’t. Tilly had never respected bedding as a concept. Her empty roll mat lay near the far wall beneath the banner they’d put up for tomorrow morning’s donor breakfast: RESILIENCE: LIVE IT. Underneath, someone had drawn a small willy in pencil on the exclamation mark. I should have got up immediately. That is the version I’d like to report officially: brave Nell Harper, loyal as a golden retriever, springing into action with sanitary products and moral certainty. What actually happened was I sat there for maybe thirty seconds thinking: no. No, because she didn’t get to ignore me for six months and then summon me with emojis. No, because if she was in the toilets with Freya or Maddie or whoever had replaced me, they could help. No, because last time Tilly needed me, I’d been useless in a way that still made my skin go hot when I remembered it. No, because I was tired. No, because I was scared she’d look at me like I was something she’d scraped off her shoe. My phone buzzed again. Same sender. same code. 🩸🩸🩸 Then, after a pause: drama That was it. One word. No punctuation. Tilly had once sent me fourteen texts about a disappointing hash brown. If she was using one word, something was wrong. I sat up. My sleeping bag made the loudest nylon shriek in human history. “Shh,” hissed someone. “Sorry,” I whispered automatically, because even in emergencies I had been raised by women who apologised to furniture after bumping into it. I reached into my rucksack and began packing. Pads. Wipes. Spare black leggings, slightly too small and with a suspicious shiny bit on one knee. Hoodie. Half a KitKat, because blood sugar was important and also because chocolate was cheaper than emotional competence. I hesitated, then added my tiny bottle of hand sanitiser and a safety pin. Mum had trained me for emergencies the way other parents trained children for piano exams. Someone always needs something, Nell. Pads. Tissues. Change for the bus. A charger. A lie that sounds believable but not too interesting. At the staff table, Miss Pryce’s head dipped forward. I crawled between sleeping bags. This was more difficult than films suggested because in films nobody had to step over Dylan Matthews snoring face-down in a Peppa Pig blanket for a dare. My sock caught on someone’s rucksack zip. I froze. Nobody moved except Callum, who muttered, “Wasn’t me,” in his sleep. By the double doors, a display board showed smiling stock-photo teenagers under the words: YOUR VOICE MATTERS. Someone had stuck a real student’s detention slip over “VOICE.” Saint Bart’s had become an academy two years ago, which meant they’d painted one wall teal, replaced the headteacher’s title with “Principal,” and started putting abstract nouns on everything. Respect. Aspire. Belong. Achieve. They never solved a problem if they could laminate it instead. I eased open the sports hall door. The corridor outside was colder and smelled faintly of bleach. Behind me, the sports hall breathed and rustled. Ahead, the school stretched out in darkness: trophy cabinets, locked classrooms, motivational vinyls peeling at the corners. The motion-sensor lights flicked on one by one as I moved, making me feel like a criminal in a very underfunded museum. My emergency phone showed 9% battery. I texted Tilly: coming Three dots appeared. Vanished. Appeared again. No reply. I walked faster. The drama studio was in the old block, past music, past the toilets everyone avoided because one cubicle door had never locked and the hand dryer sounded like a jet engine swallowing gravel. The old block had once been part of some church hall, apparently, before Saint Bart’s expanded into it like mould. At night it felt less like a school and more like the place schools went after death. Halfway there, I nearly turned back. Not properly. Just enough for my body to slow down. Just enough to imagine climbing back into my sleeping bag and pretending the phone had died before I saw anything. That was the awful thing about cowardice: it didn’t arrive wearing a cape and twiddling a moustache. It arrived sounding reasonable. Maybe she was fine. Maybe this was a prank. Maybe Tilly had meant “drama” as in emotional drama, not the actual drama studio, because Tilly liked being imprecise when it inconvenienced other people. Maybe someone else would deal with it. At the end of the corridor, the drama door was slightly open. A strip of red light showed underneath. Not bright. Not flashing. Just red, like the EXIT sign had bled down the wall. I stopped. My mouth had gone dry. “Tilly?” I whispered. Nothing. Then, from inside, her voice. Flat. Quiet. Not crying, which was worse. “Before you do your face,” she said, “it wasn’t me.” I pushed the door open.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished opening chapter that demonstrates genuine command of voice, tone, and the specific texture of British YA fiction. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: The first-person narration from Nell is consistently rendered and genuinely distinctive. The voice is dry, self-aware, and specific without being relentlessly quippy. Lines like "even in emergencies I had been raised by women who apologised to furniture after bumping into it" feel earned rather than performed. The internal monologue during the hesitation sequence — the list of "No, because..." reasons — is particularly strong. It reveals character through accumulation rather than declaration, and crucially it doesn't let Nell off the hook. The self-awareness about her own cowardice ("That is the version I'd like to report officially") is exactly the kind of honest, slightly uncomfortable narration the plan called for. BRITISH SPECIFICITY: The chapter delivers on the plan's promise of texture. The sports hall details (Peppa Pig sleeping bag, Callum Teague's fart, the phone box shrine, the detention slip over "VOICE") feel genuinely observed rather than generic. The academy-trust satire — "They never solved a problem if they could laminate it instead" — lands cleanly. The motivational vinyl critique is sharp without being laboured. DIALOGUE: Sparse but effective. Tilly's single line at the end — "Before you do your face, it wasn't me" — is excellent: it establishes character, creates immediate tension, and withholds information in exactly the right way. The "Wasn't me" running gag from Callum is a small but well-deployed comic beat. METAPHOR AND PROSE QUALITY: Most metaphors land. "Battery life of a mayfly" is good. "The school stretched out in darkness... like a criminal in a very underfunded museum" works. The "mould" expansion metaphor for the school is apt. There are a couple of moments where the prose reaches slightly — "the place schools went after death" is a touch overwrought — but these are minor. The "strip of red light... like the EXIT sign had bled down the wall" is genuinely effective foreshadowing. PACING AND STRUCTURE: The chapter does exactly what it should: establishes setting, introduces the protagonist's voice and flaw, delivers the inciting incident, and ends on a perfect hook. The pacing is controlled. The hesitation sequence before Nell acts is the emotional core of the chapter and it's handled with real skill — it's not just delay, it's characterisation. WEAKNESSES: The Tilly backstory (the Year 8 period incident) is told rather efficiently, but there's a slight risk of over-explaining the code's significance. The chapter is perhaps slightly long for what it accomplishes structurally, though the length is justified by the quality of the voice work. The "old block felt like the place schools went after death" line is the one moment where the prose tips into mild purple territory. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows the plan closely and faithfully. All the specified elements are present: the sports hall setting, the phone box, the hidden Samsung, the 🩸🩸🩸 text, the hesitation, the packing sequence, the corridor journey, and the cliffhanger ending with Tilly's line. The emotional beats match the plan's intentions precisely. OVERALL: This is a high-quality opening chapter that successfully establishes voice, character, setting, and stakes. It's funny where it should be funny, uncomfortable where it should be uncomfortable, and it ends at exactly the right moment. The writing is confident and specific without being showy. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2 — The Wrong Sort The drama studio looked like Macbeth had exploded. This was not completely unusual. Bea Ashcroft, drama captain and local dictator of all things involving props, had been rehearsing her devised piece in here for weeks, which seemed to involve witches, climate change, and everyone crawling under a tarpaulin while whispering about capitalism. There were fake daggers on the windowsill, plastic goblets in a crate, a cauldron made from a recycling bin, and three bottles of stage blood lined up beside the sound desk like very niche squash. The unusual part was Ollie March lying on the floor. Ollie March did not lie on floors. Ollie March stood at lecterns. Ollie March shook governors’ hands. Ollie March appeared in newsletters wearing a blazer and the expression of a boy who had never once forgotten PE kit. He was head boy, rugby captain, predicted all 8s and 9s, and could make teachers laugh by saying things that would have earned Jay Malik a behaviour point. Now he was on the black Macbeth tarp, one arm bent under him, blood dark in his sandy hair. Tilly stood beside him. Her hands were red. For a second, my brain refused to arrange any of this into sense. It just supplied individual facts, like flashcards. Tilly. Blood. Ollie. Tarp. Me, holding a packet of Always Ultra Night like an idiot at a sacrifice. Then Tilly said, “Close the door.” I did, because apparently my body was taking orders while my brain hid under a desk. “What happened?” I asked. My voice came out too small. Tilly looked at the pad packet in my hand. “You brought wings.” “You sent the code.” “Yeah, well. Situation evolved.” “From period to manslaughter?” “He’s not dead.” “Is that a medical opinion or a vibe?” Her face twitched, almost a smile, then locked down again. She was very pale under her uneven red hair. There was a smear of blood along her cheek like she’d tried to brush hair away and forgotten her hand was covered. “I didn’t do it,” she said. “You said that.” “I’m saying it again because your face is being very Daily Mail.” “My face has had a shock.” “So has he.” Ollie made a wet, unpleasant little sound. Both of us froze. “Oh God,” I said. “Oh God, he’s alive.” “That was already established.” “Alive people can still die, Tilly. That is one of their main design flaws.” I dropped to my knees beside him, then immediately regretted it because there was blood on the tarp and possibly fake blood and possibly both, and my leggings made a sticky sound. Ollie’s eyes were shut. His face looked wrong without its usual smooth confidence, younger and slack. There was a cut near his hairline, bleeding more than seemed reasonable for one head. I had done first aid once in Year 9. A woman from St John Ambulance had shown us how to put someone in the recovery position while Connor Evans pretended to choke on a Haribo. I remembered almost nothing except that head wounds bled a lot and everyone had laughed at the word “compressions.” “Is he breathing?” Tilly asked. “I’m checking.” “How?” “By wishing I’d paid attention.” I leaned close. His breath warmed my cheek. “Yes. Breathing.” Relief hit, quickly followed by more panic. “We need to stop the bleeding.” “With what?” I looked down at the packet in my hand. Tilly followed my gaze. “No.” “Tilly.” “Absolutely not.” “He’s bleeding from his head.” “And these are my emergency period pads.” “I brought them.” “Because I requested period support, not because Ollie March decided to cosplay a crime scene.” “He’s still a person.” “He’s Ollie.” “Tilly.” She swore, then snatched the packet, ripped it open with her teeth, and shoved a pad at me. “If he survives, I’m invoicing him.” I pressed the pad against the cut. Blood spread through the white cotton in a dark bloom. My stomach lurched. “That is not what the advert promised,” I said. Tilly barked one horrible laugh and covered her mouth. The room smelled sweet and metallic. The fake blood had a syrupy strawberry-ish smell, like cheap sweets dissolved in paint. Real blood smelled like pennies and fear. Together they made something my body wanted to reject. “What happened?” I asked again, quieter. Tilly crouched on the other side of Ollie, arms wrapped around herself, hands held away from her hoodie. “I came to talk to him.” “At midnight. In the drama studio.” “He told me to meet him here.” “Why?” She looked away. “Tilly.” “Saints Unfiltered.” The name hit the room differently from everything else. Like someone had opened a door to February. I saw it before I could stop myself: the shaky phone video behind the science block, Tilly’s face blotchy from crying, her voice saying “stop filming,” someone laughing off-screen. The caption with little halo emojis. The comments. My thumb hovering. My thumb pressing play again. Just to check how bad it was. As if pain improved under review. I swallowed. “What about it?” “There’s another post scheduled. Morning. During the breakfast thing.” Tilly’s voice was flat. “He said he could stop it.” “Ollie runs Saints Unfiltered?” “Not runs. Moderates. Feeds it. Whatever word makes him feel less like a disease.” Ollie shifted under my hand. I pressed the pad harder. “And you confronted him?” “Yes.” “And then?” “We argued. He was being—” She stopped. Her fingers flexed. “Him. Said I made everything bigger by reacting. Said no one cared unless I gave them a performance.” “That’s vile.” “Yeah, well, put it on a badge.” “What happened after?” “The door opened.” I looked at the door. “Someone came in?” I asked. “Yes.” “Who?” “I don’t know.” “Tilly.” “I don’t know. The lights went out.” As if summoned, the strip lights above us flickered. Once. Twice. Both of us looked up. “Don’t,” I told the ceiling. The lights steadied. Tilly’s voice dropped. “There was someone by the door. Ollie turned round. He looked scared. Then the lights cut. I heard him swear. Something hit the stage block. Then he was down.” “Did someone push him?” “I don’t know.” “Did you?” Her eyes snapped to mine. “No.” I believed her. Not because Tilly was incapable of violence. Tilly once threw a glue stick at Aaron Bell so accurately it hit him in the ear from two tables away. But this was different. Her anger tonight had edges, yes, but underneath it was terror. “We have to get an adult,” I said. “No.” “He needs an ambulance.” “He needs his phone.” I stared at her. “Are you actually unwell?” “His phone has the account on it.” “His head has blood coming out of it.” “And if we leave, the phone disappears and everyone says I did this.” “Tilly, this is not the moment for evidence gathering.” “It is exactly the moment. Evidence has a habit of getting lost at Saint Bart’s.” That was true. Saint Bart’s once lost a complaint about mould in the changing rooms by claiming the email had gone to “legacy inboxes,” which sounded like a haunted filing cabinet. Still. “We get help first,” I said. “Then phone.” Tilly looked like she wanted to argue, but Ollie made another sound. A small, frightened groan. I reached for my emergency mobile with my cleanest finger. 6% battery. No signal. “Brilliant,” I muttered. “Of course. Why would a school have signal? It’s only full of children.” “We can get the phones from the box,” Tilly said. “In the sports hall. Past Miss Pryce. With me covered in blood and you looking like Carrie at parents’ evening.” Before she could answer, the lights went out. Not flickered. Out. The darkness was immediate and thick. Tilly grabbed my sleeve. I kept one hand on the pad pressed to Ollie’s head. Somewhere near the sound desk, something clicked. The drama door opened. I stopped breathing. Footsteps crossed the floor. Slow, controlled. Not trainers. Not school shoes scuffing. Adult shoes. Polished soles on old wood. A shape moved between us and the corridor light. I could not see a face, only the lower half: dark trousers, neat hem, black brogues. One shoe stepped onto the edge of the tarp, into the smear of red. The person muttered, very softly, “For God’s sake.” Not shocked. Not scared. Annoyed. Tilly’s nails dug into my arm. The figure bent near Ollie. There was the faint scrape of something being lifted from the floor. A phone case clicking against a ring, maybe. Then the footsteps retreated. The door closed. The lights came back on. Ollie was still there, breathing. The pad at his head was soaked. But the space by his right hand was empty. Tilly stared at it. “Tell me you saw that,” she whispered.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 of this story is a strong piece of YA fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Nell's first-person narration is consistently well-rendered. The flashcard metaphor ("Tilly. Blood. Ollie. Tarp. Me, holding a packet of Always Ultra Night like an idiot at a sacrifice.") is genuinely funny and character-appropriate rather than forced. Her internal voice — dry, self-deprecating, observant — matches the profile precisely. The line "My face has had a shock" is exactly the kind of thing Nell would say: deflecting with understatement. Tilly's characterisation is also strong. Her refusal to use the pads on Ollie, her flat delivery, her "Daily Mail" accusation — these feel true to the profile of someone who uses sharpness as armour. The dynamic between them crackles with history without over-explaining it. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the chapter's genuine strengths. The exchange about the pads ("If he survives, I'm invoicing him") is darkly comic without being quippy in a performative way. The rhythm of interruption and deflection feels authentic to frightened teenagers. "Put it on a badge" is a good Tilly line. The dialogue earns its lightness because it's grounded in real stakes — someone is bleeding on the floor. PLOT EXECUTION: The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The discovery of Ollie, the pad comedy, Tilly's fragmented account, the mystery figure with polished shoes — all present and correctly sequenced. The revelation of Saints Unfiltered is handled with appropriate restraint; it's introduced but not over-explained. The chapter ends on the right beat. EMOTIONAL DEPTH: The moment where Nell remembers watching Tilly's video twice is the chapter's most important passage, and it's handled with real skill. "Just to check how bad it was. As if pain improved under review." This is sharp, honest, and avoids the trap of making Nell's guilt either too noble or too melodramatic. It's the kind of self-knowledge that stings precisely because it's accurate. WEAKNESSES: A few moments veer toward the slightly over-constructed. "Like someone had opened a door to February" is a decent metaphor but feels slightly more literary than Nell's established voice warrants — it's the kind of line that draws attention to itself. The "Carrie at parents' evening" joke is good but sits at the edge of being too polished for the moment. The description of Ollie as someone who "appeared in newsletters wearing a blazer and the expression of a boy who had never once forgotten PE kit" is excellent, but the paragraph establishing him risks being slightly too long for the pace of the scene. The "legacy inboxes" joke about Saint Bart's is funny but momentarily breaks the tension in a way that feels slightly indulgent — it's a good joke in the wrong place. The mysterious figure's exit is handled well — the polished shoes, the "For God's sake," the phone disappearing — but the lights conveniently coming back on immediately after feels slightly convenient. The plan called for this, but the execution could have been slightly more unsettling. PURPLE PROSE / FORCED METAPHOR: Largely avoided. The writing is controlled. "Blood spread through the white cotton in a dark bloom" is the most overtly literary image, and it earns its place. The "pennies and fear" blood smell description is a well-worn comparison but used appropriately. COHERENCE: The chapter is internally coherent and advances the mystery cleanly. The setup for subsequent chapters (the missing phone, the mystery adult, Tilly's account) is well-laid. OVERALL: This is confident, well-crafted YA fiction that successfully balances dark comedy with genuine emotional stakes. The voice is distinctive, the characters are consistent with their profiles, and the chapter plan is faithfully executed. Minor issues with occasional over-construction and one or two jokes that slightly undercut tension prevent it from being exceptional, but it's a very strong chapter. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3 — Proper Channels “I saw shoes,” I said. “Shoes.” “Adult shoes.” “That’s not helpful, Nell.” “I’m sorry I didn’t collect a full biometric profile while hiding behind a witch cloak.” Tilly crouched where the person had bent down. Her fingers hovered over the floor, not touching. There was a clean-ish rectangle in the mess, just beside Ollie’s hand, where something had been lying. His phone, presumably. Gone now. Tilly’s face had gone hard. “See?” she said. “Evidence disappears.” “Ollie is still bleeding.” “I know.” “Do you? Because you’re staring at the floor like the floor’s going to confess.” She rounded on me. “If that phone’s gone, he wakes up and says I pushed him. Then what?” “Then we say you didn’t.” “Oh, brilliant. Nell Harper says a thing. Case closed.” I flinched, which I hated, because she noticed. Ollie groaned again. His eyelids fluttered but didn’t open. The pad pressed to his head had turned mostly red. My hand had started to cramp. “We need a teacher,” I said. “Fine.” The word came out like she’d spat it. We couldn’t leave Ollie flat on his back. Or maybe we could. I didn’t know. First aid in Year 9 had not covered “popular boy injured during possible blackmail-related confrontation in drama studio.” Between us, with a lot of panicked whispering and one moment where Tilly said, “If he wakes up and gets creepy, I’m dropping him,” we managed to roll him into the recovery position. He was heavier than he looked. People always were. Films lied about that. Films had never tried to move a semi-conscious rugby captain on a sticky tarpaulin while avoiding a fake dagger and a recycling-bin cauldron. I tucked my hoodie under the pad to keep pressure on the wound. It was my good hoodie. Or, more accurately, my least bad hoodie. Mum would ask questions. Mum had a way of asking “what’s that stain?” that made you want to confess to crimes committed before birth. “Come on,” I said. We slipped out of the drama studio into the corridor. The school felt different now. Before, it had been dark and stupid and mildly creepy. Now every locked door looked deliberate. Every motivational poster seemed to be watching us fail. YOUR VOICE MATTERS. I wanted to punch it. My emergency phone had 6% battery and still no signal. I held it up like people do in films, as if the ceiling might admire my optimism. “Nothing?” Tilly asked. “Unless we need to phone the concept of disappointment.” The drama-block first-aid kit was mounted beside the toilets under a sign saying IN CASE OF EMERGENCY. The little green box was locked with a plastic catch so stiff Tilly had to smack it with the heel of her hand. Inside were two plasters, one triangular bandage, a pair of gloves fused together in their packet, and a leaflet titled *Managing Asthma in Schools*. Tilly stared. “That’s it?” “There’s also dust.” “Safeguarding.” “Don’t start.” “I’m starting. I’ve started. I’m mid-start.” We headed towards the main corridor. The quickest route to the sports hall was through the old double fire doors near music, but when I pushed them, they didn’t move. A red light blinked above the keypad. “Locked,” I said. Tilly shoved them harder. “Why?” “Because apparently during a student wellbeing event it’s important to trap the students.” “There’ll be another way.” There was. Past music, through languages, down the corridor by the staff staircase. Longer, darker, and full of trophy cabinets containing cups won by boys who now sold vape liquid out of car boots. We walked fast. Tilly kept rubbing her hands on her hoodie, smearing the blood worse. “Stop doing that,” I said. “I can feel it.” “I know, but—” “You don’t know.” The words cut. I shut up. For about twelve steps. “What is on the post?” I asked. Tilly didn’t answer. “Tilly.” “No.” “If we’re risking expulsion, police, and possibly accessory-to-whatever, I feel like details would be traditional.” She stopped so suddenly I nearly walked into her. “You really want to know?” she said. No. Obviously not. Wanting had nothing to do with it. “Yes.” Her eyes were shiny, but her voice stayed flat. “Court stuff. Mum stuff. Messages. A video from outside the family centre. Me shouting at my dad. My sister crying. Happy?” I wasn’t. The corridor hummed above us. Somewhere in the building, pipes clicked. “He had that?” I said. “He said someone sent it in.” “And you believed him?” “I believed he enjoyed having it.” I thought of Saints Unfiltered in February. The little halo emojis. The caption: *Saint Tilly blesses us with another breakdown x*. The way people had reacted with laughing faces because that was easier than deciding it was cruel. I had not sent a laughing face under the post. I had not commented. I had watched it twice. The second time, I told myself, was to check if it was as bad as I thought. Then I’d sent Tilly: you okay? x As if kindness could be slipped privately under a door after you’d stood in the crowd outside. Tilly had never replied. Now she looked at me like she could hear all of that happening in my head. “You’re doing your guilty statue thing,” she said. “I’m not.” “You are. Your whole face goes GCSE Religious Studies.” “I’m trying to help.” “You’re trying to feel like you’re helping. Different.” I deserved that. I hated that I deserved it. A metallic clatter came from ahead. We both froze. Someone was by the vending machines near the languages corridor. A tall, wiry shape crouched on the floor, one hand inside the bottom flap, the other holding a screwdriver. “Jay?” I whispered. Jay Malik jerked up so fast he banged his head on the machine. “Absolutely not,” he said, rubbing his skull. “This is a dream. Go back to bed.” “What are you doing?” Tilly demanded. Jay looked at the screwdriver. Looked at the vending machine. Looked at us: Tilly with blood on her sleeves, me clutching an asthma leaflet and a half-empty packet of pads. “What are you doing?” “Answer first,” I said. “I’m liberating Mini Cheddars from capitalist captivity.” “At midnight?” “That’s when capitalism is weakest.” Tilly stepped closer. Jay’s grin faltered. “Is that blood?” he asked. “No, Jay, we’ve joined a beetroot cult.” His eyes moved between us. He lowered the screwdriver. “What happened?” “Ollie March is hurt,” I said. “In drama.” Jay’s expression did something complicated and quick. Surprise, yes. Fear, maybe. Something like satisfaction, immediately buried. “Hurt how?” “Head wound. Breathing. Unconscious-ish.” “Unconscious-ish is not a medical category.” “Thank you, Dr Vending Machine.” Tilly pointed at him. “Did you see anyone go into drama?” “No.” “Jay.” “I saw Ollie earlier,” he said. “Arguing near the staff stairs.” “With who?” He scratched his jaw. “Could’ve been a teacher.” “Which teacher?” “Didn’t see. Adult-shaped. Nice shoes.” My skin prickled. “Black brogues?” I asked. Jay looked at me properly then. “Maybe.” Tilly swore. “Why didn’t you say anything?” I asked. “Because people argue in this school all the time. It’s basically the curriculum.” He paused. “Also, if Ollie March has been brained, I’d prefer not to be the brown kid found with a screwdriver.” That shut us up. From the direction of the drama corridor came a sound. Not a siren, exactly. Saint Bart’s evacuation tone was too underfunded for urgency. It was a tinny electronic version of “Greensleeves,” mangled through the speakers like a Tudor ghost trapped in a microwave. Jay stared. “Is the school… singing?” “The drama alarm,” Tilly said. We ran. Back through languages, past the asthma leaflet fluttering from my hand, down the old corridor. My lungs burned. Jay was faster than both of us. Tilly kept pace by fury alone. The alarm stopped just as we reached the studio door. I shoved it open. The black tarp was still there. The fake blood. The cauldron. My hoodie, crumpled and red. But Ollie March was gone.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 of this darkly comic British YA story is a strong piece of craft that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically. VOICE AND CHARACTER The first-person narration from Nell is consistently well-handled. The voice is dry, self-aware, and specific without being relentlessly quippy. Lines like "Unless we need to phone the concept of disappointment" and "I'm sorry I didn't collect a full biometric profile while hiding behind a witch cloak" feel genuinely earned rather than performed. The humour emerges from situation and character rather than being bolted on. Nell's internal admission about watching the Saints Unfiltered post twice — "The second time, I told myself, was to check if it was as bad as I thought" — is the chapter's emotional centrepiece and is handled with real restraint and precision. The guilt is shown through specific remembered behaviour rather than announced. Tilly is sharp, controlled, and credibly angry. Her line "You're trying to feel like you're helping. Different." is excellent — it's the kind of thing that lands because it's accurate rather than merely cutting. Jay's introduction is well-managed: the screwdriver, the Mini Cheddars, the "capitalist captivity" joke, and then the sudden sobriety when he sees the blood. His line about not wanting to be "the brown kid found with a screwdriver" is the chapter's most important tonal shift — it moves from farce to something real without announcement, and the narrative respects it by letting it "shut us up." DIALOGUE The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. It's genuinely British in rhythm, interrupted, asymmetrical, and often funny without being clever-clever. The exchange about the first-aid kit ("There's also dust." / "Safeguarding." / "Don't start." / "I'm starting. I've started. I'm mid-start.") is a good example of comic escalation that still feels like real teenagers. The dialogue rarely over-explains and trusts the reader. PLOT AND PLAN ADHERENCE The chapter follows its plan closely and competently. The locked fire doors, the useless first-aid kit, the argument between Nell and Tilly that opens the old wound, Jay's introduction with the screwdriver, his admission about seeing Ollie arguing with someone near the staff staircase, the "Greensleeves" alarm, and the discovery that Ollie has vanished — all present and correctly sequenced. The chapter plan called for Jay to refuse to name who he saw until he knows whether Ollie is dead; the chapter handles this slightly differently (he says "could've been a teacher" and "didn't see") but the spirit is preserved. The emotional beat about Nell watching the post twice is handled better than the plan suggested it might be — it's more specific and more damning than "sent a weak 'you okay?'" alone, and the detail of the second viewing is genuinely uncomfortable in the right way. WEAKNESSES There are some minor issues. The line "trophy cabinets containing cups won by boys who now sold vape liquid out of car boots" is a good image but slightly overreaches — it's the kind of detail that draws attention to its own cleverness. The "GCSE Religious Studies" face joke is good but the setup ("your whole face goes") is slightly clunky. The "Tudor ghost trapped in a microwave" metaphor for the Greensleeves alarm is inventive and mostly lands, though it's the chapter's most self-consciously literary moment. The chapter's pacing is excellent until the final sprint back to the studio, which is slightly rushed — "My lungs burned. Jay was faster than both of us. Tilly kept pace by fury alone" is functional but the last clause is a little too neat, a touch of purple. The ending — Ollie gone — is effective and correctly cliffhanger-shaped, though it's exactly what the plan specified, so it's more competent execution than creative surprise. The first-aid kit scene is very funny but the joke is slightly telegraphed by the setup. The asthma leaflet returning as a prop ("the asthma leaflet fluttering from my hand") during the run is a nice callback. OVERALL ASSESSMENT This is confident, well-controlled YA writing with a strong voice, good comic timing, and genuine emotional intelligence. The chapter does what it sets out to do without feeling mechanical. The darkness and lightness are well-balanced. The characters are consistent with their profiles. The main limitation is that it's executing a plan rather than surprising us — the chapter rarely exceeds its own intentions, but it meets them with real skill. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4 — Confessions For about three seconds, nobody said anything. This was rare for Jay and medically concerning for Tilly. The drama studio hummed around us, suddenly too ordinary. Fake daggers. Plastic goblets. The witch cauldron. My hoodie lying in a dark wet twist on the tarpaulin, looking like an animal that had been run over. But no Ollie. “Right,” Jay said eventually. “On the positive side, he’s not dead.” “You don’t know that,” Tilly snapped. “Well, if he is, he’s become extremely mobile.” I stepped onto the tarp. My shoes stuck slightly. The pad we’d used on Ollie’s head lay beside the stage block, red and swollen and horribly practical. There was a smear leading away from it, across the black plastic, towards the back exit by the staff staircase. Then it stopped. Not faded. Stopped. As if someone had lifted him or he’d managed to walk carefully on air, which would be very Ollie if he could arrange witnesses. “He didn’t get up alone,” I said. Jay crouched, not touching anything. “You sure?” “No.” “Love a confident detective.” “I’m sure I’m not sure.” Tilly was staring at the empty space where Ollie had been. Her jaw was clenched so hard I could see the muscle jump. “We need his phone,” she said. “We need to find the bleeding person,” I said. “We need both.” “We are three GCSE students and one screwdriver.” “Four,” said a voice from the doorway. We all spun. Bea Ashcroft stood there in tartan pyjama bottoms, school blazer, and fluffy slippers shaped like tigers. Her hair was scraped into a bun so tight it looked load-bearing. In one hand she held a torch. In the other, a bottle of fake blood. She surveyed the room. Then she said, with quiet fury, “If anyone has got real blood on my Macbeth tarp, I am becoming a problem for this institution.” Jay pointed at her slippers. “Are those tigers?” “They’re emotionally necessary. Explain the corpse absence.” “There’s no corpse,” I said quickly. “That’s what someone with a corpse would say.” Tilly made a noise halfway between a laugh and a sob. Bea’s eyes moved to her bloody sleeves, then to my hoodie, then to the stage block. Her theatrical expression flattened into something much more useful. “Who?” she asked. “Ollie March,” I said. Bea’s eyebrows rose. “I withdraw my earlier concern about the tarp by approximately twelve percent.” “He’s hurt,” I said. “I’m not cheering. I’m recalibrating.” Tilly stepped towards her. “Did you see anyone?” “I saw the alarm trigger on the drama panel from the corridor, came to check why my set had apparently developed a nervous system, and found you lot reenacting a safeguarding failure.” Bea lifted the fake blood bottle. “Also this was in the hall. Open. Which it shouldn’t be, because stage blood stains, costs actual money, and certain people treat the arts like a free murder buffet.” “Not the main issue,” Jay said. “It’s adjacent.” Bea moved into the room, careful not to step in anything. “Start again. Quickly.” Nobody wanted to. So we did it badly, all at once. Tilly: “I came to meet Ollie—” Me: “He was bleeding but breathing—” Jay: “I was liberating snacks, unrelated—” Bea held up one hand. “Christ alive. Individually, before I stage-manage an aneurysm.” We told her enough. Not everything. Tilly kept the scheduled post vague. I noticed. Bea noticed too, but let it pass for approximately one minute, which for Bea was basically sainthood. At the words Saints Unfiltered, her face changed. “Oh,” she said. “That diseased little confessional.” “You know it?” I asked. “Everyone knows it. That’s the problem.” She set the fake blood down with exaggerated care. “Forgive me, Bart, for I have screenshotted. Sinner of the Week. Martyr Monday. Very witty if your emotional development stopped in a bin.” Jay looked at me. “She’s got the branding.” Bea ignored him. “They posted my toothpaste advert.” Despite everything, I blinked. “Your what?” “When I was nine, I auditioned for a commercial. I had excellent diction and unfortunate confidence. Someone found the video and put it up with a caption about plaque and desperation.” She lifted her chin. “I made it look intentional.” Jay squinted. “Were you the one singing ‘brush it, don’t rush it’?” Bea’s torch beam snapped to his face. “Continue that sentence and I’ll make your death look like choreography.” He shut up, impressed. Tilly folded her arms. “Ollie had something scheduled for morning.” Bea’s attention sharpened. “During the donor breakfast?” Tilly nodded. “Of course. Because nothing says student wellbeing like public digital humiliation before pastries.” Bea paced once, thinking. “Did he have his phone?” “Someone took it,” I said. “Who?” “Adult shoes,” Jay said. “Black brogues,” I added. Bea went still. “What?” I asked. “Half the male staff wear black shoes, Nell. This is not Cinderella.” “But?” “But Mr Voss came through here earlier,” she said. “Before lights-out. Checking the hall was ‘presentation ready.’ Which apparently includes telling me my witch circle was a trip hazard.” Tilly laughed without humour. “He knew.” “Knew what?” Bea asked. Tilly looked away. I said, “There’s a post. About her family.” Tilly shot me a look. “She needed to know,” I said, though my voice came out weak. Bea’s expression softened, which somehow made Tilly look angrier. “Don’t,” Tilly said. “I wasn’t.” “You were about to do sympathy with your eyebrows.” “My eyebrows are private.” Jay had wandered to the back door. “Blood stops here,” he said. “Staff stairs are through there, yeah?” The door was locked from our side, but not with a key. A keypad. Red light blinking. Bea pointed. “That goes to admin and the staff offices. Voss’s kingdom.” Tilly kicked the bottom of the door. It did not move. “Helpful,” Bea said. “Very nuanced.” “I’m open to better ideas.” “I have one,” Bea said. “Possibly.” She crossed to the sound desk, then to a tripod shoved behind a stack of chairs. An old school tablet was clipped to it, the screen dark. “I set this up to record rehearsal blocking,” she said. “For my devised piece.” “The witch capitalism thing?” Jay asked. “It’s about environmental grief and inherited guilt, but thank you for your commitment to being wrong.” “Was it recording?” I asked. “I don’t know. I thought I turned it off after rehearsal. But Geraldine sometimes glitches.” “Geraldine?” “The tablet.” “Of course.” Bea pressed the power button. Nothing. “Dead,” she said. “But if it recorded, it may have uploaded to the department cloud when it caught Wi-Fi.” “Can you access it?” “Not from here. Media room, maybe. Staff login, ideally.” Jay lifted his screwdriver. Bea looked at it. “No.” “I didn’t say anything.” “You radiated burglary.” “It’s not burglary if it’s our school.” “That is not how law works.” He shrugged. “Media room lock’s broken anyway. You kick under the handle. Big Keith told us not to, which was basically a tutorial.” Somewhere beyond the staff door, something clicked. A latch. A closing door. Close enough that all of us stopped breathing. Bea turned off her torch. In the dark, Tilly found my sleeve and gripped it once. Hard. Then she let go.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 of this story is a strong piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTERISATION The first-person narration from Nell is consistently well-handled. Her voice is dry, observational, and self-aware without being relentlessly quippy. Lines like "I'm sure I'm not sure" and "We are three GCSE students and one screwdriver" feel genuinely earned rather than performed. The humour emerges from character and situation rather than from a joke machine. Bea's introduction is the chapter's strongest achievement. She arrives in tiger slippers with a torch and a bottle of fake blood, and her characterisation is immediately distinct: theatrical in manner but practical in crisis, furious about the tarp before she is furious about the injury. "I withdraw my earlier concern about the tarp by approximately twelve percent" is exactly the kind of line that works because it reveals character rather than just being clever. Her naming the tablet "Geraldine" and her threat about making Jay's death "look like choreography" are consistent with the profile and land well. Jay's voice is also well-maintained. His deflection through jokes ("Love a confident detective"), his screwdriver-as-burglary-tool, and his casual knowledge of the broken lock all feel true to the character profile. His line about Ollie becoming "extremely mobile" is the right kind of dark comedy for the situation. Tilly is perhaps the least developed in this chapter, which is partly appropriate given her emotional state, but her moments — gripping Nell's sleeve in the dark, the "don't do sympathy with your eyebrows" exchange — are sharp and consistent with her profile. The detail that she keeps the scheduled post vague, and that Nell notices, is a nice piece of character work. DIALOGUE The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element overall. The overlapping exposition scene ("Tilly: I came to meet Ollie— / Me: He was bleeding but breathing— / Jay: I was liberating snacks, unrelated—") is a genuinely funny structural choice that also feels true to how teenagers would actually communicate under stress. Bea's "Christ alive. Individually, before I stage-manage an aneurysm" is a perfect response to it. The exchange about the toothpaste advert is well-handled: it reveals character, advances the Saints Unfiltered subplot, and generates comedy without feeling like an info-dump. Jay's "Were you the one singing 'brush it, don't rush it'?" and Bea's response are the chapter's funniest moment and feel genuinely earned. The dialogue occasionally tips into being slightly too polished — characters are a little too consistently witty even under stress — but this is a minor complaint given the genre and the overall quality. PLOT AND STRUCTURE The chapter follows its plan closely. Ollie's disappearance is established, Bea is introduced with her props and her own Saints Unfiltered grievance, the drama tablet as potential evidence is introduced, and the chapter ends on a tense note with the sound of a closing door. The pacing is good: the comedy and the threat are balanced without either undermining the other. The detail of the blood smear stopping abruptly — "Not faded. Stopped. As if someone had lifted him or he'd managed to walk carefully on air, which would be very Ollie if he could arrange witnesses" — is a nice piece of writing that does double duty as observation and character commentary. The chapter does what it needs to do structurally: it widens the world of Saints Unfiltered through Bea's grievance and the mention of others, advances the mystery, and sets up the media room sequence. The ending — the click in the dark, Tilly gripping Nell's sleeve and then letting go — is restrained and effective. EMOTIONAL DEPTH The chapter is lighter on emotional depth than some others in the plan, which is appropriate given its function as a widening-out chapter. The brief moment where Tilly shoots Nell a look after Nell reveals the post about her family, and Nell's weak "she needed to know," is the chapter's most emotionally honest beat. It shows Nell's tendency to justify small betrayals to herself, which is central to her character arc. The Tilly-Nell tension is present but not overplayed. The "sympathy with your eyebrows" exchange is both funny and genuinely revealing about Tilly's defensive relationship with pity. WEAKNESSES The chapter's main weakness is that it occasionally feels slightly too controlled — the characters are almost too consistently funny and quick-witted for people who are frightened and sleep-deprived. The plan called for "dialogue that allows awkwardness, interruption, silence, and failed jokes," and while there is some of this, the chapter leans more heavily on successful jokes than failed ones. The metaphor "my hoodie lying in a dark wet twist on the tarpaulin, looking like an animal that had been run over" is effective. The opening "This was rare for Jay and medically concerning for Tilly" is a good comic beat. There are no significant instances of purple prose or forced metaphor. The chapter is not amateurish. The craft is evident throughout. The British school texture is present (the keypad, the staff staircase, the department cloud, the broken lock that Big Keith warned them about). The comedy is specific rather than generic. FAITHFULNESS TO PLAN The chapter follows its plan closely. Bea is introduced with her props and her grievance. The tablet as potential evidence is established. The Saints Unfiltered world is widened. The chapter ends with the sound of a closing door. The plan's note about Nell and Tilly's fight being "ugly, not therapeutic" is partially delivered — the tension is present but the full clash is deferred, which is probably the right call for pacing. The plan mentioned that "Nell snaps back something unfair about Tilly enjoying drama, and instantly regrets it" — this does not appear in the chapter, which is a minor deviation. The plan also mentioned Maya being referenced; she is not mentioned here, though this may be appropriate given the chapter's focus. Overall, this is a well-executed chapter that delivers on most of its promises. The voice is consistent, the comedy is earned, the characters are distinct, and the plot advances meaningfully. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5 — Receipts The media room was halfway down the English corridor, wedged between a cupboard full of broken tripods and a display on “Oracy Champions” that had been peeling since November. The door had a keypad lock, a laminated sign saying AUTHORISED STAFF ONLY, and, according to Jay, “a weak spiritual core.” “Do not kick it,” Bea whispered. Jay lifted one foot. “Jay.” “I’m assessing.” “With your trainer?” “It’s a multi-sensory assessment.” Tilly shoved past both of them, pressed the handle, and the door opened. Jay stared. “That was going to be my next move.” Inside, the media room was dark and warm and smelled of dust, plastic, and the ghosts of overheated projectors. Computers lined the walls. A green screen drooped in one corner like a depressed vegetable. On a shelf sat three cameras, two missing their battery covers, and a label maker with PROPERTY OF GEOGRAPHY scratched into the side. Bea went straight to the main computer. “If anyone touches anything with jam on their hands, I’ll know.” “My hands are mostly blood,” Tilly said. “That’s not the loophole you think it is.” I found tissues in my bag and handed them to Tilly. She took them without looking at me. That somehow felt more intimate than if she had cried. The computer woke slowly, making the sort of grinding noise I associated with dentists and school printers. A login screen appeared. “Password?” I asked. Bea typed. Wrong. She typed again. Wrong. Jay leaned over. “Try Drama123.” Bea gave him a look that could have preserved meat. “It’s always Subject123,” he said. “IT teacher told us not to use it in Year Eight and half the staff visibly panicked.” Bea typed Drama123. Wrong. “Ha,” she said. The screen flashed: password hint — *favourite musical + year*. Bea shut her eyes. “If it’s LesMis2020 I’m transferring.” It was LesMis2020. Jay spread his hands. “MI5.” The desktop loaded, then immediately announced it needed updates. “No,” Bea said, with the calm of someone speaking to a dog holding a knife. “Not now.” The loading circle spun. Tilly paced behind us, rubbing at her fingers with the tissue. The blood had gone brownish at the edges. My hoodie was still in the drama studio. I suddenly remembered Mum had bought it in the sale and said, “This one’s decent, don’t destroy it,” which seemed almost rude of her in hindsight. Finally the folders appeared. Bea clicked through Drama, Recordings, Macbeth, Devised, Final_Final, Final_Final_ACTUAL, and a folder labelled DO NOT DELETE OR I WILL KNOW. “There,” she said. The file had uploaded at 00:08. We all went quiet. Bea pressed play. At first there was only a sideways view of the drama floor, black tarp cutting diagonally across the frame. Someone had set the tablet badly, or Geraldine had slumped. You could see the bottom of the stage block, one fake dagger, and a lot of dust. Then voices. Ollie, clear as anything: “You’re making this bigger than it is.” Tilly: “You have a video of my sister crying.” Ollie: “I don’t have anything. People submit stuff.” Tilly: “Then delete it.” Ollie laughed. Not loudly. Worse. Softly, like she was being embarrassing. “You love this,” he said. “You love being tragic.” Beside me, Tilly went completely still. My stomach tightened. On-screen, only shoes moved. Tilly’s scuffed black ones. Ollie’s polished school shoes. He stepped into frame, then out again. Tilly’s voice shook now. “Delete it.” “Or what?” A door creaked. Ollie said, “Sir?” Then the lights in the video cut. The audio kept going. A scuffle. Tilly saying, “What are you—” Ollie swearing. A hard crack that made all of us flinch even though we had already seen the result. Then silence. Then my own blood turned cold. Mr Voss’s voice, low and furious: “Oliver. Give me the phone.” Tilly, panicked: “He’s bleeding.” Voss: “Not here. Not tonight.” The file ended. Nobody spoke. The computer fan whirred like it was embarrassed.
